OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones. Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Nail–patella syndrome, Coffin–Siris syndrome, Meier–Gorlin syndrome, Coxopodopatellar syndrome, Genitopatellar syndrome
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient with HIV and hepatitis C coinfection (likely incidental).  
- Known hepatic haemangiomas, incidental finding of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior iliac bones.  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with luxation on knee X-rays.  
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature on elbow X-rays.  
- Hypoplastic fingernails observed on physical exam.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome:  

Pros: Classic features include nail dysplasia (hypoplastic fingernails), patellar aplasia or hypoplasia with subluxation or dislocation, and elbow abnormalities (radial head hypoplasia and radio-humeral dislocation). Bilateral iliac wing processes (or lateral costochondral or iliac wing dysplasia) can be present. This syndrome exactly fits the described findings.  

Cons: None significant; matches well.

2) CoffinâSiris syndrome:

Pros: Features include nail hypoplasia (typically of the fifth digit), limb anomalies.

Cons: Primarily involves nail hypoplasia, absent distal phalanges, and psychomotor retardation; there is no typical patellar or radial head involvement. Skeletal issues are more digits and hand anomalies, not patellar or radial head hypoplasia. Iliac wing processes not typical.

3) MeierâGorlin syndrome:

Pros: Includes limb and nail abnormalities.

Cons: Predominantly characterized by short stature, limb anomalies like preaxial and postaxial polydactyly, hypoplasia of nails and distal phalanges, but not associated with patellar hypoplasia or radial head abnormalities. Also includes midline defects and orodental anomalies. Iliac wing processes not typical.

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:

Pros: Features include patellar hypoplasia or absence.

Cons: Usually presents with severe intellectual disability, cataracts, and significant craniofacial anomalies; limb anomalies include patellar hypoplasia, but radial head hypoplasia and iliac processes less typical. Nail dysplasia may be present but less common. Usually bilateral and severe, but radiographic specifics less matching.

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:

Pros: Involves patellar agenesis/hypoplasia, along with other skeletal anomalies.

Cons: Usually associated with severe developmental delay, genitourinary anomalies, facial dysmorphism, and renal anomalies. The triad includes patellar hypoplasia but not commonly radial head hypoplasia or iliac processes. Nail dysplasia not prominent.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Nailâpatella syndrome: Exact match for all three major findings (nail hypoplasia, bilateral patellar hypoplasia with dislocation, bilateral radial head hypoplasia with dislocation), and bilateral iliac wing processes (osseo-projections) are also typical.  
2) Genitopatellar syndrome: Patellar hypoplasia fits, but nail hypoplasia is not a major feature, and iliac processes not typical.  
3) Coxopodopatellar syndrome: Patellar hypoplasia present, but radial head and nail findings less typical.  
4) MeierâGorlin syndrome: Nail hypoplasia and limb anomalies but specific radial and patellar findings absent.  
5) CoffinâSiris syndrome: Nail and digital anomalies present, but patellar and radial findings absent.  

Thus, Nailâpatella syndrome best fits all findings.

</analysis>

<final>   
Nailâpatella syndrome   
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base. Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed. It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe. A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Elderly male, 81 years old, with history of atrial fibrillation and new onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea.  
- Elevated troponins, third-degree AV block, right ventricular thickening, and pericardial effusion on echocardiogram.  
- PET-CT shows large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue extending through right-sided chambers (right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, atrioventricular groove), sparing left ventricle, and a hypermetabolic lung nodule at left lung base.  
- Contrast-enhanced chest CT shows infiltrative hypodense myocardial mass in the same right-sided structures, causing mural thickening, encircling right coronary artery, with moderate pericardial effusion.  
- CT-guided biopsy of lung nodule taken for histology.  
- Imaging pattern includes large, hypermetabolic myocardial infiltration, predominantly right-sided chambers, with additional lung nodule.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  

1) Angiosarcoma  
- Pros: Angiosarcomas often arise in right atrium and may infiltrate right chambers; hypermetabolic on PET; can cause pericardial effusion.  
- Cons: Usually primary cardiac tumor causing intracardiac mass, but lung metastasis or synchronous lung nodule seen less frequently; still plausible.  

2) Ig-G4 related disease  
- Pros: IgG4-RD can cause fibroinflammatory pseudotumors; can involve pericardium; may cause cardiac masses; can involve multiple sites.  
- Cons: Rarely causes hypermetabolic masses mimicking malignancy; usually less hypermetabolic; typical distribution more perivascular and fibrosis rather than large infiltrative myocardial mass. Uncommon to have lung nodules.  

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma  
- Pros: Often presents with right-heart involvement; hypermetabolic on PET; can cause pericardial effusion; can involve multiple chambers; can metastasize or have synchronous nodules; elderly immunocompetent patients can be affected; presentation consistent with large myocardial infiltration.  
- Cons: Less common than sarcomas but plausible; typically involves right chambers; pericardial effusion common.  

4) ErdheimâChester disease  
- Pros: Multisystemic infiltrative disease, can involve heart and pericardium; can form masses around vessels (right coronary artery cuff); pericardial effusion possible.  
- Cons: Usually does not form large hypermetabolic myocardial masses; typically causes fibrotic infiltrates rather than bulky masses; lung nodules uncommon; PET may be hypermetabolic but myocardium infiltration pattern less likely bulky mass.  

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma  
- Pros: Cardiac sarcoma can involve right heart; hypermetabolic on PET; can cause large myocardial masses and pericardial effusion.  
- Cons: Usually rare; lung metastasis may occur but simultaneous lung primaries less common; sarcoma often heterogeneous masses with necrosis; imaging less specific.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Primary cardiac lymphoma - best fits right atrial/ventricular and interatrial septum involvement, hypermetabolic PET mass, and lung nodule (could represent metastatic or synchronous disease). Pericardial effusion and mural thickening typical. Age and presentation consistent.  
2) Angiosarcoma - close second given right atrial predominance, hypermetabolic mass, and pericardial effusion, but lung nodules less common; would require metastasis or second primary.  
3) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma - possible but less likely given lung nodule separate and less typical presentation.  
4) Erdheim-Chester disease - less likely due to bulky myocardial infiltration pattern and lung nodule.  
5) Ig-G4 related disease - least likely due to hypermetabolic mass and lung nodule.  

<final>Primary cardiac lymphoma</final><|return|>
options: Angiosarcoma | Ig-G4 related disease | Primary cardiac lymphoma | Erdheim–Chester disease | Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.
PostDescription:

Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein. Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus. Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed, revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

FinalDiagnosis: Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young male (37 years) with systemic inflammatory signs (fever, chills, elevated TLC, neutrophilia) and abdominal pain in epigastrium.  
- Imaging shows hepatomegaly with a sizable left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) with signs of impending rupture.  
- Doppler USG shows absent flow at porto-splenic confluence, indicating thrombosis, and cavernous transformation of the portal vein (collaterals at porta hepatis).  
- Intrahepatic ductal dilatation in left lobe and obstruction distal to common hepatic duct on MRCP.  
- CECT confirms abscess, thrombosis, and pinpoint narrowing of IVC with a small segment thrombus.  
- Pancreatic parenchymal varices involving head and body of pancreas draining into splenic vein.  
- Intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals noted between branches of hepatic veins.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
   - Pros: Portal venous thrombosis, cavernous transformation, varices are compatible.  
   - Cons: No mention of typical signs of cirrhosis (nodular surface, altered liver contour, fibrosis, splenomegaly); large liver abscess is not a classic presentation. Patient does not have risk factors or signs of chronic liver disease.  

2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
   - Pros: Intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals could suggest abnormal vascular connections.  
   - Cons: No direct evidence of abnormal arterial flow or classic AVM imaging features; main pathology is abscess and portal/splenic vein thrombosis, not vascular malformation.  

3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Portal vein thrombosis (pylephlebitis) is present, associated with liver abscesses; cavernous transformation of portal vein is consistent.  
   - Pancreatic parenchymal varices explained as collateral formation due to portal venous blockade.  
   - Acute presentation with infection and thrombosis fits pylephlebitis.  

4) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
   - Pros: None stated.  
   - Cons: No imaging features of cystic lesions; pancreatic findings are varices, not cystic pathology.  

5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms  
   - Pros: None suggested.  
   - Cons: No evidence of pancreatic ductal dilation or cystic mass typical for IPMNs, no mucin-related findings.  

C) Ranking: 3 > 1 > 2 > 5 > 4  
- The presence of pylephlebitis with liver abscess and resultant pancreatic varices secondary to portal vein thrombosis is the most cohesive and explanatory diagnosis.
- Liver cirrhosis is less likely without classic features of chronic liver disease.
- AVM is not well supported by imaging.
- Cystic lesions and IPMNs are unfounded based on imaging and clinical context.  

<final>  
Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
</final><|return|>
model_answer: Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess
options: Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation | Arteriovenous malformation (AVM) | Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess | Cystic lesions of pancreas | Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 31-year-old male with a large solid and cystic/necrotic testicular mass on the right hemiscrotum.
- No normal right testicular or epididymal parenchyma visualized.
- Normal left testis and epididymis.
- Extremely high alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) levels >53,000 µg/L.
- No metastasis or lymphadenopathy on CT.
- Ultrasound shows vascularized heterogeneous mass with necrotic areas.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Mixed germinal cell tumour
   - Pros: AFP can be elevated in mixed germ cell tumors (contains multiple histologic components, including yolk sac, embryonal carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, etc.).
   - Cons: Usually, mixed germ cell tumors have variable AFP levels and other markers like beta-hCG may be elevated depending on components. No info on beta-hCG. Large necrotic areas and vascularization fit, but no metastasis could argue against extensive spread despite size.

2) Pure yolk sac tumour
   - Pros: Yolk sac tumors are known to produce extremely high AFP levels (AFP is a hallmark marker). Vascularization and necrotic areas are common. Typically occurs in children/young adults, but can be seen in older age too. No metastasis does not rule out due to early stage or size.
   - Cons: Yolk sac tumors often large when discovered, but metastasis is common. No metastasis present but not absolute contra.

3) Seminoma
   - Pros: Common testicular tumor, usually presents as a solid mass, can be vascular.
   - Cons: Seminomas typically do not elevate AFP; normal AFP rules out this diagnosis (AFP is negative or normal in seminoma unless it has mixed components, but none mentioned). AFP >53,000 is far too high for seminoma.

4) Choriocarcinoma
   - Pros: Choriocarcinoma often produces high beta-hCG, can be hemorrhagic/necrotic.
   - Cons: Does not produce AFP; AFP is not a marker for choriocarcinoma. Elevated AFP excludes this.

5) Teratoma
   - Pros: May have cystic and solid components; heterogeneous.
   - Cons: Typically does not produce AFP unless mixed with yolk sac or embryonal carcinoma components. Usually occurs in younger patients. Not associated with extreme AFP elevation alone.

6) Lymphoma
   - Pros: Can involve testis, often bilateral, heterogeneous mass.
   - Cons: Lymphoma does not raise AFP levels; it is a hematologic malignancy and would be negative for AFP. Not consistent with AFP >53,000 µg/L.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
- Seminoma and choriocarcinoma and lymphoma are excluded due to AFP elevation incompatibility.
- Teratoma is unlikely primary diagnosis due to AFP extremely high; teratoma alone does not raise AFP.
- Mixed germ cell tumor could have very high AFP if yolk sac component present, but usually multiple markers may rise.
- Pure yolk sac tumor is the most specific and is a germ cell tumor in the adult that secretes very high AFP, fitting the extremely high AFP level >53,000 µg/L perfectly.
- Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is Pure yolk sac tumour.

<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>
options: Mixed germinal cell tumour | Pure yolk sac tumour | Seminoma | Choriocarcinoma | Teratoma | Lymphoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left
 
 hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI
 
 revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d). Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted (Figure 1d).
 
 CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left (Figure 2).
 
 CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid
 
 internal carotid arteries (
 
 ICAs) (Figure 3). The left
 
 middle
 
 cerebral artery (
 
 MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left
 
 anterior cerebral artery (
 
 ACA) was perfused through the
 
 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (
 
 PCA) was identified.
 
 Digital subtraction angiography (
 
 DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance (Figures 4a and 4b) and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale. Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted. CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left. CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries (ICAs). The left middle cerebral artery (MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left anterior cerebral artery (ACA) was perfused through the anterior communicating (ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA) was identified. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events, Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement, Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia, ICA dissection causing ischemia, Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 39-year-old male with sudden onset left hemiparesis and mild speech slurring, suggestive of right hemispheric involvement.  
- MRI shows acute watershed infarcts in right centrum semiovale.  
- Ivy sign on MRI (sulcal hyperintensity in right temporal region), indicating slow or stagnant flow in superficial veins, often seen in chronic cerebral hypoperfusion.  
- CT shows hypoplastic carotid canal on the left.  
- CTA reveals left ICA hypoplasia, bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenosis, left middle cerebral artery supplied via posterior circulation, left anterior cerebral artery supplied via anterior communicating artery.  
- Presence of foetal origin of left PCA.  
- DSA confirms these findings and shows no "puff of smoke" appearance or collateral formation.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events:  
   - Pros: Bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenosis can be caused by atherosclerosis.  
   - Cons: Bilateral stenoses in a young person (39) without other risk factors are less typical; absence of risk factors is not explicitly listed but implied by limited data; the presence of left ICA hypoplasia and carotid canal hypoplasia is developmental, not atherosclerotic. Ivy sign suggests chronic hypoperfusion rather than acute atherosclerotic events.  

2) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement:  
   - Pros: Bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenosis, ivy sign, and ischemic events in watershed zones are compatible.  
   - Cons: There is no "puff of smoke" collateral formation on DSA, which is a hallmark feature of Moyamoya syndrome. Lack of collaterals rules against Moyamoya.  

3) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia:  
   - Pros: Could explain bilateral ICA stenoses and chronic hypoperfusion signs.  
   - Cons: Absence of characteristic moyamoya collaterals ("puff of smoke") on DSA excludes Moyamoya disease. ICA hypoplasia can be part of Moyamoya but collaterals are necessary for diagnosis. Hence this is ruled out.  

4) ICA dissection causing ischemia:  
   - Pros: Acute focal stenosis of ICAs might be explained by dissection.  
   - Cons: Bilateral involvement and hypoplastic carotid canal on CT strongly support a congenital rather than acquired arterial pathology; dissection typically unilateral and acute with typical imaging signs (intimal flap, false lumen), not described here. Additionally, ICA dissection does not explain left ICA hypoplasia or the hypoplastic carotid canal.  

5) Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries:  
   - Pros: Vasculitis can cause multifocal stenoses and ischemic strokes.  
   - Cons: No mention of systemic vasculitis symptoms or inflammatory markers; hypoplastic carotid canal and ICA hypoplasia point towards a congenital etiology rather than an inflammatory process; presence of ivy sign suggests chronic hypoperfusion rather than acute vasculitic changes.  

C) Ranking and tie-break:  
1) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement: Not correct due to lack of moyamoya collaterals.  
2) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia: Ruled out due to absence of collaterals.  
3) ICA dissection causing ischemia: Unlikely due to bilateral stenosis and hypoplasia.  
4) Vasculitis-induced stenosis: No clinical/inflammatory evidence; hypoplastic carotid canal contradicts inflammatory etiology.  
5) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events: By exclusion, most plausible, as the bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenoses (in a young adult) and ischemic events match, though congenital hypoplasia suggests an atypical case. However, given the absence of collaterals for Moyamoya and no evidence for dissection or vasculitis, the best fit among given options is atherosclerotic ICA stenosis. Typically, atherosclerosis is rare this young without risk factors, but it's the closest match.  

Hence, the chosen diagnosis is "Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events."
</analysis>  
<final>  
Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events  
</final><|return|>
model_reasoning_raw:
A) Key findings:  
- 39-year-old male with sudden onset left hemiparesis and mild speech slurring, suggestive of right hemispheric involvement.

- CT shows hypoplastic carotid canal on the left.  
- CTA reveals left ICA hypoplasia, bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenosis, left middle cerebral artery supplied via posterior circulation, left anterior cerebral artery supplied via anterior communicating artery.

   - Cons: There is no "puff of smoke" collateral formation on DSA, which is a hallmark feature of Moyamoya syndrome. Lack of collaterals rules against Moyamoya.

   - Pros: Could explain bilateral ICA stenoses and chronic hypoperfusion signs.  
   - Cons: Absence of characteristic moyamoya collaterals ("puff of smoke") on DSA excludes Moyamoya disease. ICA hypoplasia can be part of Moyamoya but collaterals are necessary for diagnosis. Hence this is ruled out.

4) Vasculitis-induced stenosis: No clinical/inflammatory evidence; hypoplastic carotid canal contradicts inflammatory etiology.  
5) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events: By exclusion, most plausible, as the bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenoses (in a young adult) and ischemic events match, though congenital hypoplasia suggests an atypical case. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 51-year-old female patient, a farmer, presented with two years history of abdominal pain, vomits, diarrhoea, asthenia, weight loss (12%), and arthralgias. Laboratory findings showed
 
 iron deficiency anaemia
 
 , increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, C-reactive protein 6 mg/dL
 
 (normal value < 0.5 mg/dL)
 
 and hypoalbuminemia 2.5 g/dL.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystadenocarcinoma of the right ovary with
 
 suspicion of lymph node metastases
 
 and the patient was referred to our oncology institute.
 
 The exams were re-assessed and the malignant ovarian tumour was excluded, as the MRI was highly suggestive of cystadenofibroma
 
 (Figure 1).
 
 In addition, the CT demonstrated low-attenuation lymph nodes (fatty infiltration) in the left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum and mesentery
 
 (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 3a, and 3b). Furthermore, prominent jejunal folds were seen on T2-weighted images (Figure 4).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 51-year-old female patient with chronic abdominal symptoms (pain, vomiting, diarrhoea), systemic signs (asthenia, weight loss, arthralgias).  
- Lab abnormalities: iron deficiency anemia, elevated ESR, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, high CRP, hypoalbuminemia.  
- Imaging findings: Initially suspected right ovarian cystadenocarcinoma with possible lymph node metastases, but MRI later suggested cystadenofibroma (benign).  
- Importantly, CT showed low-attenuation lymph nodes in multiple regions (left supraclavicular, mediastinum, retrocaval, mesenteric) showing fatty infiltration (hypodense on CT).  
- MRI showed prominent jejunal folds on T2-weighted images (suggesting small bowel involvement).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Whipple's disease: Typically presents with malabsorption, weight loss, and abdominal pain, often with thickened small bowel loops/trophozoites in biopsy. The prominent jejunal folds support but no mention of characteristic findings such as lymphadenopathy with low attenuation (fatty infiltration). Whipple's disease usually does not cause fatty infiltration of lymph nodes.  
  - Pros: Abdominal pain, weight loss, malabsorption signs.  
  - Cons: Lymph node appearance (fatty infiltration) not typical; no mention of classic small bowel mucosal lesions or Whipple's histology.  
- Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease: This syndrome is associated with celiac disease and is characterized by low-attenuation (fatty) lymph nodes on CT in the mesentery and other regions, often associated with prominent jejunal folds consistent with celiac sprue. Systemic symptoms like weight loss, arthralgias, anemia, and elevated inflammatory markers are consistent with celiac disease.  
  - Pros: Fatty infiltration of lymph nodes (cavitating), prominent jejunal folds (consistent with celiac sprue), systemic features typical for celiac disease, multiple lymph node locations.  
  - Cons: No mention of confirmed celiac serology or biopsy; but imaging and clinical picture strongly suggest it.  
- Lymphoma: Can cause lymphadenopathy, but fatty infiltration of lymph nodes is uncommon. Also, lymphoma would more often present as solid lymph nodes rather than low-attenuation (fatty) nodes. No mass or nodal enlargement described.  
  - Pros: Lymphadenopathy in multiple regions could fit lymphoma.  
  - Cons: Fatty infiltrated nodes unusual for lymphoma; no mass lesions; no other typical lymphoma symptoms.  
- Malignant lymph nodes: Same as above but specifically malignant cells; initial suspicion due to ovarian lesion, but later excluded. Fatty infiltration argues against malignancy.  
  - Pros: Initial suspicion of malignant nodes.  
  - Cons: Fatty infiltration is not a feature of malignant nodes; imaging later excluded malignancy.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease  
   - Rationale: Highly suggestive imaging findings (fatty lymph nodes in multiple regions, prominent jejunal folds), clinical picture consistent with celiac disease; fatty infiltration of lymph nodes is characteristic.  
2) Whipple's disease  
   - Rationale: Some overlapping symptoms, but imaging findings less typical; no fatty lymph nodes.  
3) Lymphoma  
   - Rationale: Lymphadenopathy possible but fatty infiltration uncommon; no mass described.  
4) Malignant lymph nodes  
   - Rationale: Excluded based on imaging and follow-up.  

<final> Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease </final><|return|>

options: Whipple’s disease | Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease | Lymphoma | Malignant lymph nodes

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.
PostDescription:

There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass. When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged. It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment. An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations. Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 40-year-old man with a right ischiorectal fossa mass.
- Initial CT shows a fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left.
- Mass is surrounded by a thin capsule, no enhancing soft tissue, fluid, or calcifications.
- Five years later, mass enlarged significantly.
- Contains two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU).
- Irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
- MRI shows mix of fluid and soft tissue centrally with thin and thick septations.
- Thick, irregular rim of enhancement around the entire mass.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1. Extragonadal germ cell tumour
   - Pros: Can occur in midline/pelvic locations; can present as mass.
   - Cons: Typically solid, may have necrosis or calcifications, but not usually a fat attenuation lesion or well-defined cystic compartments. Unlikely to be fat attenuation and to initially present as a simple fat lesion.

2. Ischiorectal liposarcoma
   - Pros: Can arise in ischiorectal fossa; may contain fat attenuation components; can have cystic or necrotic areas.
   - Cons: Usually solid with variable fat and soft tissue attenuation; initial presentation without enhancing soft tissue or fluid unlikely for liposarcoma; may have calcifications; typically more aggressive with larger size.

3. Dermoid cyst
   - Pros: Fat-containing cystic lesion; common in sacrococcygeal/perineal region; can have cystic compartments; may enhance rim.
   - Cons: Dermoid cysts often contain fat, fluid, sometimes calcifications (tooth enamel or bone); here no calcifications were noted initially; but could be compatible.

4. Tailgut cyst
   - Pros: Midline or paramedian cystic masses in presacral or perineal region; can be multiloculated; may have thick walls and enhancing rims especially if infected or inflamed.
   - Cons: Typically does not contain fat attenuation; usually cystic with fluid or mucinous contents; fat attenuation mass less typical.

5. Perineal epidermal cyst
   - Pros: Can be cystic lesions in perineum; usually have fluid attenuation; may have thick rim.
   - Cons: Usually not fat attenuation; typically no fat component; doesn't explain initial fat attenuation mass.

6. Anterior sacral meningocele
   - Pros: Typically cystic lesion in presacral region; cystic with fluid.
   - Cons: Usually not fat attenuation; does not displace anal canal; usually communicates with spinal canal; enhancement pattern different; no fat component expected.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
1) Dermoid cyst: best fits fat attenuation mass, cystic compartments, and thick irregular enhancing rim. Dermoid cysts are known to contain fat and can be multiloculated with complex contents. No calcifications were noted, but not required.
2) Ischiorectal liposarcoma: less likely given initial lesion was purely fat attenuation without soft tissue enhancement, and slow progression with cystic changes.
3) Tailgut cyst: less likely due to absence of fat attenuation and location not classic.
4) Extragonadal germ cell tumour: less likely due to imaging characteristics.
5) Perineal epidermal cyst: less likely due to fat attenuation.
6) Anterior sacral meningocele: less likely based on location and imaging features.

<final>Dermoid cyst</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 55-year-old female with follicular lymphoma, systemic symptoms (weight loss, fever), and cervical lymphadenopathy.
- Large heterogeneous lesion (7.5 x 5 cm) in the right hepatic lobe with fluid densities on CT.
- Biopsy was non-diagnostic (fibrohyalinized nodule).
- MRI findings: heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with thin wall.
  - Hyperintense on T1 and hypointense on T2.
  - Radiating septa to periphery: septa hypointense on T2, hyperintense on T1, delineating multiple smaller cysts (hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2).
  - No restricted diffusion.
  - No significant enhancement of soft tissue component after contrast.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros and cons:

1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb)
   - Pros: Large cystic hepatic lesion with daughter cysts (multiple smaller cysts), thickened walls, and internal septations are classic features. The fluid density on CT, cystic nature on MRI with T1 hyperintensity and T2 hypointensity could be due to hydatid cyst contents (proteinaceous or hemorrhagic fluid). Radiating septa delineating smaller cysts are typical for hydatid cysts with daughter cysts.
   - Cons: No mention of typical hydatid signs like calcifications or the "water lily sign." However, the imaging description matches well with complex cystic liver lesions with daughter cysts typical for hydatid type IIb cysts.

2) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis
   - Pros: Patient has follicular lymphoma, which can involve the liver and may present with necrotic cystic metastases.
   - Cons: Metastases usually show some enhancement, restricted diffusion, and may not show the type of internal septations and daughter cyst-like structures. The lesion did not enhance or show restricted diffusion, making metastasis less likely.

3) Hepatic abscess
   - Pros: Infectious/inflammatory process could show T1 hyperintensity if proteinaceous; weight loss and fever might fit.
   - Cons: No restricted diffusion, no significant enhancement of soft tissue components, and no mention of perilesional inflammatory changes. Also, biopsy showed fibrohyalinized nodule not supporting abscess. Abscesses usually show restricted diffusion and wall enhancement.

4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma
   - Pros: Cystic hepatic neoplasms can present as complex cystic lesions with internal septa. Young female patient fits the epidemiology.
   - Cons: These lesions usually enhance the cyst wall and septa after contrast, and often show mural nodules. No significant enhancement was seen here. Also, T1 hyperintensity is less typical unless there is hemorrhage or proteinaceous fluid.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) – matches the description best, especially radiating septa and daughter cysts.
2) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma – possible but less likely due to lack of enhancement and typical features.
3) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis – less likely due to lack of enhancement and restricted diffusion.
4) Hepatic abscess – least likely given imaging and biopsy findings.

<final> Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) </final><|return|>
options: Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) | Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis | Hepatic abscess | Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

On the US exam, the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

- Humeral head cranially displaced with narrowing of subacromial space.  
- Osseous degenerative changes in humeral head and ACJ.  
- Ultrasound: Supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, erupting superiorly from the AC interval with no Doppler signal.  
- Rotator cuff interrogation shows absence of supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over humeral head, plus bony contour irregularity.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury  
   - Pros: Mass near ACJ; humeral head displacement.  
   - Cons: No trauma history; fluid collection suggests chronic process; absence of tendons suggests chronic rotator cuff tear, not acute trauma.  
2) Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes  
   - Pros: Degenerative changes noted in humeral head and ACJ.  
   - Cons: Does not explain soft tissue mass or absent rotator cuff tendons or the fluid collection; does not account for mass-like lesion.  
3) Acromioclavicular cyst  
   - Pros: Soft-tissue mass near ACJ, fluid collection consistent with a cyst; consistent with AC joint area; no trauma required.  
   - Cons: Does not explain absent supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons; cyst can be primary or secondary.  
4) Soft-tissue tumour  
   - Pros: Hard, non-mobile mass in elderly patient could be a tumour.  
   - Cons: Imaging shows homogeneous soft-tissue density mass; fluid collection on US suggests cyst rather than solid tumour; absence of Doppler signal argues against vascularised tumour.  
5) Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear  
   - Pros: Absence of supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons indicates massive rotator cuff tear; fluid collection from AC interval; ACJ location of mass; cranial displacement of humeral head (superior migration) consistent with rotator cuff arthropathy; cystic fluid collection consistent with secondary cyst formation due to massive cuff tear.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.

 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm
 
 (ap/axial/cc) (
 
 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (
 
 small arrow in Figure 2
 
 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-
 
 enhancing
 
 fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe (arrows on Figure 3).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient: 9-year-old female child.
- Symptoms: Fever, restricted neck movements, posterior bulging of the mucosa in the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal area, lateralized to the left.
- Imaging: Contrast-enhanced CT neck.
- Findings: Well-defined oval fluid collection with rim enhancement in the retropharyngeal space (2 x 2 x 1 cm). Retropharyngeal space edema from the skull base to hypopharynx. Thin well-defined rim-enhancing fistulous tract from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe.
- Piriform sinuses inspection normal.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Retropharyngeal abscess
   - Pros: Fluid collection with rim enhancement and surrounding edema is typical of an abscess. Restricted neck movements and fever support infection.
   - Cons: The presence of a fistulous tract connecting to the thyroid lobe is not typical for a simple abscess and suggests a connection to another structure.

2) Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst
   - Pros: Branchial cleft cysts can become infected and present as fluid collections with rim enhancement. The fistulous tract could reflect a congenital tract.
   - Cons: Branchial cleft cysts are usually lateral rather than purely retropharyngeal and more commonly cystic with no fistulous tract to the thyroid; the location is atypical for third/fourth branchial cleft cysts, which are generally lateral neck, not retropharyngeal.

3) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy
   - Pros: Necrotic lymph node can appear as a fluid collection with rim enhancement and adjacent edema in the retropharyngeal space. Fever and restricted neck movements support infection/necrosis.
   - Cons: Fistulous tract to thyroid lobe not typical for necrotic lymph nodes; location is consistent but fistula suggests another pathology.

4) Suppurative thyroiditis
   - Pros: Fistulous tract connecting retropharyngeal space to the thyroid lobe suggests spread of infection or direct connection to thyroid. Suppurative thyroiditis can present with fever, neck pain, limited motion.
   - Cons: The primary lesion is in the retropharyngeal space, not initially in the thyroid. However, fistulous connection implies involvement of the thyroid gland or a tract from another structure.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:

1) Presumed pathognomonic: The fistulous tract connecting the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the thyroid lobe strongly suggests a direct connection between these two sites. This is unusual for simple retropharyngeal abscess or necrotic lymph nodes. Branchial cleft cysts typically track along embryological remnants and can form fistulas, often to the pharynx or skin, but connection to thyroid is more characteristic of fourth branchial cleft anomalies. However, third/fourth branchial cleft anomalies commonly involve the piriform sinus, which was normal here.
- Retropharyngeal abscess is a common cause but rarely has fistulous tracts to thyroid.
- Necrotic lymph nodes unlikely to form fistulous tracts.
- Suppurative thyroiditis alone would involve the thyroid primarily; here the abscess appears primary in the retropharyngeal space with a fistulous tract to thyroid, suggesting infectious spread from retropharyngeal space to thyroid, i.e., suppurative thyroiditis secondary to retropharyngeal abscess.

Thus the best diagnosis must combine the abscess and the fistula to thyroid; this combination fits with suppurative thyroiditis secondary to a retropharyngeal infection, but the imaging shows a primary retropharyngeal collection with a fistula to thyroid. The fistulous tract to the thyroid lobe is the distinctive and pathognomonic feature, pointing to suppurative thyroiditis with a fistulous connection from a retropharyngeal abscess.

Ranking: 
1) Suppurative thyroiditis (due to fistulous tract to the thyroid)
2) Retropharyngeal abscess with fistulization to thyroid (still essentially suppurative thyroiditis or retropharyngeal abscess complication)
3) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy (less likely due to fistula)
4) Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst (less likely due to location and normal piriform sinus)

<final> Suppurative thyroiditis </final><|return|>
options: Retropharyngeal abscess | Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst | Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy | Suppurative thyroiditis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Two-day-old male with pyloric atresia (congenital anomaly) and cutaneous lesion.  
- Liver US shows normal biliary tree and two hypoechoic liver lesions.  
- Lesion 1 in segment IVb enlarges significantly from 6 mm to ~34 mm in 2 days.  
- CEUS and MRI show lesion 1 is T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular shape with capsular retraction, no enhancement on imaging or diffusion restriction.  
- Lesions 2 and 3 are smaller, T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity, also no enhancement or diffusion restriction.  
- No fat, no macroscopic enhancement; dynamic imaging shows no perfusion.  
- Lesion with infiltrative appearance and capsular retraction is unusual in neonates.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hepatoblastoma  
   - Pros: Most common malignant hepatic tumor in children; can be hyperechoic or hypoechoic; can be large and infiltrative.  
   - Cons: Typically show arterial phase enhancement and washout; usually no capsular retraction; lesions often have solid enhancement with possible calcification; in neonates very rare, and 2-day-old neonates seldom present with hepatoblastoma.  

2) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can present as hypoechoic lesions, may infiltrate liver.  
   - Cons: Very rare in neonates; usually show strong enhancement; typically larger and more diffuse lesions; absence of enhancement and diffusion restriction less typical; clinical scenario less supportive (no systemic symptoms, no lymphadenopathy).  

3) Infantile myofibromatosis  
   - Pros: Known to present with cutaneous lesions at birth; often multiple skin or subcutaneous nodules, sometimes internal involvement including liver; can be hypoechoic on US; lesions often hypovascular or avascular on imaging studies; can enlarge rapidly.  
   - Cons: Liver involvement often presents as multiple well-defined lesions rather than a large infiltrative lesion with capsular retraction; however, variability exists.  

4) Neuroblastoma  
   - Pros: Can metastasize to liver; can present in neonates; hypoechoic lesions; often heterogeneous.  
   - Cons: Usually associated with primary adrenal or paraspinal mass; enhancement typically seen; patient has no known adrenal mass or typical systemic signs; lesions in liver usually metastases but would expect multiple and hypervascular lesions; no enhancement seen is atypical.  

5) Haemangiomata  
   - Pros: Common benign liver lesion in neonates; often hypoechoic or hyperechoic; can be multiple.  
   - Cons: Usually shows arterial-phase enhancement with rapid washout on imaging; typically no capsular retraction; lesion 1 is infiltrative and without enhancement, not typical of haemangioma; lesion growth from 6 to 34 mm in 2 days unusual for haemangioma.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Infantile myofibromatosis  
   - Reason: The patient has a cutaneous lesion which was biopsied and the liver lesion is hypoechoic, avascular (no enhancement), rapidly enlarging, with no typical enhancement pattern. This presentation fits well with infantile myofibromatosis, which can have visceral involvement (including liver) and is known for cutaneous and subcutaneous nodules. The lack of enhancement and diffusion restriction support a benign, fibroblastic tumor rather than malignancy. Capsular retraction can occur in fibrous tumors.  
2) Lymphoma  
   - Reason: Possible but unlikely given age and imaging features without enhancement.  
3) Hepatoblastoma  
   - Reason: Unlikely given age (2 days old is extremely early), lack of arterial enhancement, and capsular retraction.  
4) Neuroblastoma  
   - Reason: No primary tumor identified; no enhancement; less likely.  
5) Haemangiomata  
   - Reason: Lack of enhancement and infiltrative nature with capsular retraction less typical for haemangioma.  

Final choice: Infantile myofibromatosis</final><|return|>
options: Hepatoblastoma | Lymphoma | Infantile myofibromatosis | Neuroblastoma | Haemangiomata

 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.

However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast. This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres to around 22x15 mm, and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures, with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Hypervascular lung metastases, Pulmonary sequestration, Bronchocoele, Pulmonary varix, Pulmonary arterial aneurysm, Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations, Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient with known malignant renal mass undergoing staging CT.  
- Multiple lesions with tissue-like densities, mostly peripheral in both lung fields.  
- Sizes ranging from a few mm up to 22x15 mm.  
- These lesions enhance during the arterial phase similar to pulmonary vascular structures.  
- Feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hypervascular lung metastases:  
   pros: patient has known malignancy; lesions are hypervascular.  
   cons: metastases usually do not have clearly identified feeding arteries and venous drainage like vascular malformations; they appear as solid masses rather than vascular structures with feeding and draining vessels.  

2) Pulmonary sequestration:  
   pros: can be hypervascular and have systemic arterial supply.  
   cons: typically congenital, isolated segment of lung receiving systemic (aortic) arterial supply and no connection to pulmonary arteries or veins; drainage usually via systemic veins; not typical in this setting and usually localized rather than multifocal bilateral lesions; the feeding vessels are from systemic arteries, not lobar pulmonary arteries.  

3) Bronchocoele:  
   pros: vascular enhancement with bronchial feeding is possible.  
   cons: bronchocoeles are airway dilatations filled with mucus; do not correspond to vascular lesions with feeding arteries and draining veins as described.  

4) Pulmonary varix:  
   pros: vascular structure, venous dilation.  
   cons: venous structures dilated but usually have normal pulmonary arterial supply and venous drainage; they are not typically arterial enhancing or fed by lobar arteries. Usually solitary and not multiple bilateral lesions.  

5) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm:  
   pros: vascular lesion with arterial feeding and venous drainage; can enhance in arterial phase.  
   cons: aneurysms are focal dilatations of arteries, less likely to be multiple smaller lesions; usually solitary and not multiple peripheral nodules.  

6) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations:  
   pros: bilateral, multiple, peripheral vascular-like lesions; feeding arteries from pulmonary arteries and drainage into pulmonary veins; enhance in arterial phase similar to vascular structures; matches the classic description of AVMs.  
   cons: none significant; findings are typical for AVMs.  

7) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications:  
   pros: abnormal vessels connecting systemic and pulmonary circulation.  
   cons: usually involve systemic arterial supply, not the pulmonary arteries; the description specifies feeding vessels from lobar arteries (pulmonary arteries).  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations - best match to imaging findings (multiple peripheral vascular lesions with feeding pulmonary arteries and draining pulmonary veins).  
2) Hypervascular lung metastases - second, but lacks vascular feeding and drainage pattern.  
3) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm - less likely due to size and multiplicity.  
4) Pulmonary sequestration - feeding arteries from systemic not pulmonary arteries, and usually localized.  
5) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications - similar to sequestration but no systemic arterial supply described.  
6) Pulmonary varix - typically venous dilation only, not multiple nodular lesions.  
7) Bronchocoele - not a vascular lesion.  

Thus, the imaging findings are diagnostic of pulmonary arteriovenous malformations.  
</analysis>  
<final> Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture, and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT. Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain. The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Calcific tendinitis, Neurogenic heterotopic ossification, Myositis ossificans, Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease, Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva, Tumoral calcinosis, Sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis> 
Key findings: A 39-year-old man with tibial/fibular fractures sustained in an MVA and TBI. Six months later, while bedridden, he develops shoulder pain. Imaging reveals extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint with mature ossification. The patient had no new trauma or systemic disease.

Candidate pass/fail:
1) Calcific tendinitis - typically causes localized calcifications in tendons, often in rotator cuff, but does not produce extensive bilateral mature heterotopic bone in joints, especially no bridging ossifications. Not consistent with mature heterotopic bone described. Fail.
2) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification - classic in patients with TBI or spinal cord injury, particularly mature heterotopic bone in joints, often hips and shoulders. The patient had a TBI, was bedridden, and developed mature bilateral heterotopic ossification in shoulders, fitting well. Pass.
3) Myositis ossificans - localized to muscle, usually after trauma, typically unilateral and more focal. This case shows bilateral, mature, extensive joint heterotopic bone in a neurogenic context. Also, myositis ossificans is more acute/subacute, not six months later with mature bone. Fail.
4) Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease - these are unlikely; no systemic signs or lab findings described; imaging shows heterotopic ossification, not typical for these disorders. Fail.
5) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva - a genetic condition causing progressive heterotopic ossification often with malformed great toes and early onset childhood; does not fit the acute trauma and TBI setting. No mention of congenital deformities or progressive course. Fail.
6) Tumoral calcinosis - massive, painless periarticular calcium deposits, not mature heterotopic bone formation in joints. Imaging shows ossifications within joints, not just periarticular calcifications. Fail.
7) Sarcoma - malignant bone or soft tissue tumor, would be suspected in unusual aggressive lesions; heterotopic ossification seen is mature and benign-appearing, no periosteal reaction or soft tissue mass. Unlikely. Fail.

Ranking:
1) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification (pass, classic for TBI and joint ossification)
2) Myositis ossificans (fail due to location and timing)
3) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (fail due to age and no congenital features)
4) Calcific tendinitis (fail)
5) Sarcoma (fail)
6) Tumoral calcinosis (fail)
7) Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease (fail)

Thus, the best fit is neurogenic heterotopic ossification.

</analysis>

<final>Neurogenic heterotopic ossification</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.
PostDescription:
