OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old male with a history of glioblastoma, submitted to surgical resection followed by Stupp protocol 11 years ago, and organic psychosis, with therapeutic noncompliance and multiple hospitalisations in recent years, presented to the emergency department with acute onset gait disturbance, right-sided muscle weakness, numbness and behavioural changes.
 Imaging Findings: A head CT performed upon admission revealed a status post left parietal craniotomy, with a cortico-subcortical temporoparietal surgical site exhibiting gliosis, stable when compared to previous imaging. No new hypodensities suggestive of stroke were observed, and CT angiography excluded arterial occlusion.
 
 Subsequent brain MRI T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated
 
 de novo
 
 cortical gyriform enhancement in the previously treated surgical site, specifically in the temporoparietal region, with extension into the occipital, insular and remaining temporal lobe. Perfusion arterial spin labelling (ASL) study indicated increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the affected area. Importantly, there were no signs of acute stroke nor tumour progression, as evidenced by the absence of restricted diffusion, an extension of the high T2 FLAIR signal or nodular enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 26-year-old female with no relevant past medical history presented with a two-year history of worsening pain at the right proximal humerus and associated weakness.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the right shoulder (Figures 1a and 1b) showed an expansile lucent proximal humeral meta-diaphysis lesion with well-defined medial and craniocaudal margins, narrow zone of transition, mild lateral endosteal scalloping and subtle medial periosteal reaction.
 
 CT of the right shoulder (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a predominantly fat-density soft tissue mass and an extensive area of full-thickness cortical loss anteriorly with focal permeation of the posterior cortex of the meta-diaphysis.
 
 Subsequent MRI (Figures 3, 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) confirmed a 4.8 cm maximum diameter predominantly fat-signal lesion within the proximal humerus with mildly enhancing internal curvilinear soft tissue stranding and more nodular soft tissue superiorly with speckled intra-lesional fat and extensive full thickness cortical loss and medial cortical permeation with secondary stress fracture. Mildly enhancing mild peri-lesional marrow and cortical oedema were also noted. There was no extra-osseous invasion.
 
 Nuclear medicine whole-body scan revealed avid uptake at the right proximal humerus with no other disease.

The predominant fat-density lesion with cortical involvement and stress fracture, with only mild soft tissue component, suggests an intraosseous lipoma with possible fracture and mild periosteal reaction. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-month-old girl was brought to casualty with a history of intermittent fever for 1 month, abdominal distension, and straining while passing urine and stools for 4 days. On examination, a soft, tender, non-mobile midline mass was palpated in the suprapubic region.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal greyscale ultrasound revealed a large, well-defined cystic lesion in the pelvis with dense mobile echoes (Figure 1). The mass was seen posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum and was extending into the abdominal cavity up to the umbilicus. The cystic structure was in continuity with the stretched uterine cavity (Figure 2). Suprapubic probe tenderness was positive. There was no evidence of any solid component or calcifications within. Both the ovaries were seen separately from this lesion. Bilateral kidneys showed mild hydronephrosis. Bowel loops were normal in course, calibre, and peristalsis and were displaced to the periphery. On transperineal ultrasound examination, there was a thin membrane of 1 mm thickness at the vaginal orifice (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A middle-aged patient presented with what were initially suspected to be new-onset migraines but then developed auditory and visual hallucinations and complex paranoid beliefs. They became confused and intermittently incoherent with deteriorating coordination, becoming unsteady and sustaining several falls. The clinical picture was further complicated by the onset of myoclonus and a rapidly progressive cognitive decline, which raised suspicion for a neurodegenerative condition.
 Imaging Findings: The patient initially underwent a CT which showed only generalised cerebral atrophy.
 
 They then had further investigations with an MRI, including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) images. This revealed widespread restricted diffusion involving almost the entire cortex except the superior temporal gyrus and also affecting the caudate bilaterally and the left lentiform nuclei. Fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) imaging showed a more subtle high signal in these areas. Other than some small and non-specific foci of high signal in the deep white matter, there were no further positive findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old woman presented with worsening perineal pain and pustular skin lesions. Examination revealed extensive erythematous and pustular lesions in the perineal and perianal regions, with rope-like dermal thickening and induration. Multiple fistulous openings with purulent and haemoserous discharge were noted.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the perineal and perianal regions revealed dermal and hypodermal band-like structures indicative of fistulas/tunnels, with multiple dermal collections around hair follicles containing hyperechoic hair fragments (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). Some hypodermal fluid collections were connected to the epidermis via sinus tracts (Figure 1d). Power Doppler showed increased vascularity, suggesting active inflammation (Figure 1e).
 
 MRI demonstrated skin thickening at the perineum and labia majora, hypertrophic scarring, and deep subcutaneous fistulas/tunnels extending into the ischioanal space and perianal region (Figures 2a and 2b), with left adductor muscle involvement suggestive of myositis (Figure 2c). The tunnels exhibited post-contrast rim enhancement, indicating active inflammation, but no fistulas communicating with the anal canal lumen were identified.
 
 After immunomodulatory therapy, significant lesion reduction was observed, with resolution of the fistulas and myositis. Residual skin thickening and scarring were noted, with minimal post-gadolinium enhancement, indicating minor active inflammation (Figures 3a and 3b).

- Post-treatment: lesion reduction, resolution of fistulas and myositis, residual skin thickening and scarring with minimal enhancement (minor residual inflammation).

1) Crohn’s disease  
- Pros: Common in perianal fistulas and abscesses; fistulas can extend to ischioanal space; inflammatory changes possible.  
- Cons: Usually fistulas communicate with anal canal (absent here); rash and pustular lesions less typical; no gastrointestinal symptoms mentioned; typically involves rectal area rather than perineal/hair follicle origin.  
2) Hidradenitis suppurativa  
- Pros: Classic chronic inflammatory disease of apocrine gland-bearing skin areas (including perineum/perianal), presenting with painful, erythematous nodules, abscesses, sinuses, and fistulas; rope-like dermal thickening and induration typical; presence of hair follicle involvement with hyperechoic hair fragments supports follicular origin; scarring, myositis, and extension into deep spaces are known complications; skin lesions with pustules; no fistula communication with anal canal; improvement with immunomodulatory therapy consistent with HS management.  
- Cons: None significant; findings fit well.  
3) Perianal fistula  
- Pros: Fistulous tracts are present.  
- Cons: Usually arise from anorectal abscesses or anal canal origin; here no fistulous communication with anal canal; lesions originate from skin/dermis rather than anorectal mucosa; clinical context and skin changes favor another diagnosis.  
4) Fournier gangrene  
- Pros: Severe necrotizing fasciitis of perineal region; could present with erythema, pain.  
- Cons: Typically acute, rapidly progressive, with systemic toxicity; imaging would show gas or extensive soft tissue necrosis, not multiple slow-growing sinus tracts or fistulas; no mention of systemic symptoms or gas; clinical course over months more fitting of chronic disease.  
5) Carbuncles  
- Pros: Collections of furuncles presenting as swollen, red areas; may have sinuses and pus.  
- Cons: Usually superficial and localized, not causing deep sinus tracts, fistulas, or deep inflammation and myositis; no rope-like thickening or scarring; chronic deep sinus tracts not typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Hidradenitis suppurativa (fits best with chronicity, follicular hair fragment involvement, rope-like dermal thickening, sinus tracts, myositis, no anal canal communication, and treatment response).  
2) Crohn’s disease (potentially, but lack of anal canal fistulas and follicular/hair involvement less typical).  
3) Perianal fistula (does not explain skin lesions, follicular involvement, no anal canal communication).  
4) Fournier gangrene (acute and necrotizing, not matching chronic presentation).  
5) Carbuncles (superficial, no deep sinus tracts or myositis).  
Thus, Hidradenitis suppurativa is most consistent with clinical and imaging findings.

</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male with a history of traumatic brain injury as an infant with subsequent hydrocephalus status post ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt. Shunt revision was done 2 years back. Elevated blood pressure was noted in the outpatient department and referred to emergency.
 Imaging Findings: CT head demonstrated ventriculomegaly (Figure 1a) compared to the baseline CT one year back (Figure 1b).
 
 The shunt series, including anteroposterior radiographs of the skull, neck, chest, and abdomen, showed a left-sided VP shunt coursing along the neck and chest with the peritoneal part coiled within the right upper quadrant. A large globular density was noted in the right upper quadrant overlying the liver shadow. Prior abandoned shunt is also visualised (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The right upper quadrant ultrasound showed a fluid collection displacing the liver posteriorly (Figure 3).
 
 CT abdomen demonstrated a subcapsular collection of fluid with scalloping of the liver parenchyma. The distal shunt coiled and terminated within the cyst (Figures 4a and 4b).
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient: 20-year-old male with traumatic brain injury, hydrocephalus, and VP shunt placement with revision 2 years ago.
- Clinical: Newly noted elevated blood pressure, referral to emergency.
- Imaging: CT head shows ventriculomegaly compared to baseline, suggesting shunt malfunction or ineffective CSF drainage.
- Shunt series radiographs show left-sided VP shunt with peritoneal tip coiled within right upper quadrant.
- Large globular density on right upper quadrant overlying liver shadow.
- Ultrasound shows fluid collection displacing liver posteriorly.
- CT abdomen confirms subcapsular fluid collection with scalloping of liver parenchyma.
- Distal shunt tip is coiled and terminates within this cyst.

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1â2 pros/cons each:
1) Hepatic subcapsular cerebrospinal fluid pseudocyst
   - Pros: CT shows subcapsular fluid collection with scalloping of liver parenchyma; globular density over the liver shadow; distal shunt terminates within cyst; these are classic findings for subcapsular CSF pseudocyst.
   - Cons: Requires distinction from simple ascites or peritoneal pseudocyst; but location subcapsular and relation to shunt tip argue in favor.
2) Localised ascites
   - Pros: Fluid collection could be misinterpreted as fluid.
   - Cons: Ascites is usually free fluid in peritoneal cavity, not subcapsular and scalloping liver parenchyma; shunt tip within cyst would not be explained.
3) Peritoneal cerebrospinal fluid pseudocyst
   - Pros: Similar pathology but typically intraperitoneal free fluid or cystic collection not subcapsular.
   - Cons: The collection is clearly subcapsular hepatic and not standard peritoneal pseudocyst; cyst location subcapsular is key; plus scalloping of liver parenchyma seen; the distal shunt tip within cyst supports subcapsular location.

C) Ranking:
1) Hepatic subcapsular cerebrospinal fluid pseudocyst: Matches subcapsular location and scalloping of liver; shunt tip within cyst.
2) Peritoneal cerebrospinal fluid pseudocyst: Location mismatch (subcapsular vs peritoneal cavity).
3) Localised ascites: Does not explain subcapsular cyst or shunt tip within lesion or scalloping of liver.

Therefore, the best diagnosis is hepatic subcapsular CSF pseudocyst.

</analysis><final>Hepatic subcapsular cerebrospinal fluid pseudocyst</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old male presented to the outpatient clinic with swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, which progressively grew after a fall four months before. He had a previous history of neoadjuvant radiotherapy followed by resection of a soft tissue sarcoma in the left thigh seven years before.
 Imaging Findings: On the preoperative MRI performed seven years before (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 2c), an intramuscular lobulated mass was seen in the left tensor fascia lata, extending into the subcutis. The mass showed areas of high signal on T1WI (Figure 2a), which became hypo-intense after fat suppression (Figure 2b). The mass was heterogeneous, predominantly hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 2b) and showed internal areas of heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium contrast (Figure 2c).
 
 The current MR study revealed a thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, with heterogeneous high signal on T1WI (Figures 3a and 3b) and T2WI (Figures 4a and 4b), persisting after fat suppression. Multiple internal fluid-fluid levels were present. While post-contrast T1WI sequences (Figure 5) showed intralesional heterogeneous high signal, the dynamic subtraction sequence (Figure 6), only showed a thick peripheral enhancing rim. Therefore, the pseudoenhancement seen on the static sequences was due to a haemorrhage-related high native T1WI signal.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>

- Patient age: 52-year-old male.
- Clinical presentation: Swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, growing after a fall 4 months prior.

- Imaging findings (current study): Thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, heterogeneous high signal on T1WI and T2WI, persistent after fat suppression (excludes fat content), multiple internal fluid-fluid levels (suggestive of hemorrhagic or venous blood products), post-contrast T1WI shows intralesional heterogeneous high signal, but dynamic subtraction only shows thick peripheral rim enhancement (the "pseudoenhancement" is due to hemorrhage-related high native T1 signal rather than true enhancement).

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Morel-Lavallée lesion in a patient with previous myxoid liposarcoma:
   - Pros: Morel-Lavallée lesions are post-traumatic closed degloving injuries that present with collections containing fluid-fluid levels (hemorrhage and seroma), and well-defined, thick-walled lesions superficial to fascia may be seen. The lesion is superficial and lobulated.
   - Cons: Patient had a history of previous sarcoma, but that lesion was on the left thigh, whereas this lesion is right-sided. Though Morel-Lavallée lesions can be post-traumatic (patient had a fall 4 months prior), the previous sarcoma was on the left side and does not directly explain the new lesion.

2) Metastasis of myxoid liposarcoma:
   - Pros: Patient has myxoid liposarcoma, which is known for frequent extrapulmonary metastases, especially to soft tissues and bones. Lesion shows heterogeneous T1/T2 signals and internal enhancement.
   - Cons: Presence of fluid-fluid levels is less typical for metastasis. Metastases generally enhance solidly rather than thin rim enhancement with internal hemorrhage. Also, metastases are rarely superficial and lobulated as described. The lesion shows hemorrhage and a thick peripheral rim only on dynamic imaging, which is less typical for metastasis; metastasis would usually show solid enhancement.

3) Angiosarcoma:
   - Pros: Angiosarcoma is a vascular tumor that can have hemorrhagic components (blood-filled spaces), fluid-fluid levels due to hemorrhage, and can appear heterogeneously enhancing. Angiosarcomas are aggressive and may have necrotic/cystic areas with hemorrhage.
   - Cons: Angiosarcoma is rare here without direct evidence. The lesion is superficial with thick rim enhancement, not typical for an aggressive soft tissue angiosarcoma which usually shows more solid enhancement. Also, the patient's main history points more towards myxoid liposarcoma recurrence/metastasis or trauma-related lesion.

4) Haematoma:
   - Pros: Haematoma can show fluid-fluid levels, high T1 signal due to subacute blood, thick-walled lesion with peripheral enhancement due to surrounding granulation tissue or capsule. Could be post-traumatic (the fall) and in the appropriate location.
   - Cons: Usually appears with no solid mass effect on dynamic imaging and no internal enhancement beyond peripheral rim, matching the dynamic images here. However, a lesion in a previous sarcoma patient could be a hematoma, given the trauma history.

C) Ranking:
1) Haematoma: This lesion's imaging characteristics best match a hematoma (fluid-fluid levels, high T1 signal from hemorrhage, thick rim enhancement only on dynamic subtraction). The history of a fall 4 months prior supports this.
2) Morel-Lavallée lesion: This is similar to a hematoma but specifically a closed degloving injury. The subcutaneous location, lesion thickness, and fluid-fluid levels could fit, but the history and timing are less clear. The previous sarcoma was on the left side, making this less likely.
3) Angiosarcoma: While hemorrhage and enhancement could fit, the thin rim enhancement and lack of solid enhancing nodules argue against it.
4) Metastasis of myxoid liposarcoma: While possible given past sarcoma, the enhancement pattern and hemorrhagic content with fluid-fluid levels are atypical.

Given the specific imaging features (fluid-fluid levels, thick-walled lesion, hemorrhage-related high T1 signal, no solid internal enhancement on dynamic imaging), and trauma history, haematoma appears most consistent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old female, with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion, had a CT head and cervical spine in 2007 for unrelated symptoms. Incidentally, imaging identified a heavily calcified lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The patient remained asymptomatic and, therefore, opted for regular follow-up imaging as opposed to surgical management.
 Imaging Findings: The CT brain demonstrated a 2.5 cm (maximal diameter), highly calcified intra-axial lesion with no surrounding oedema or mass effect, within the right cerebellar fossa (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 The MRI demonstrated the same lesion within the right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring 2.5 cm (maximal axial measurement). The lesion abutted the inferior tentorial surface; however, a broad base against it was not exhibited. There was no notable mass effect or surrounding oedema. On T1 (Figure 3) and T2 (Figure 4) weighted images, the lesion displayed a low signal intensity. Additionally, blooming artefacts were observed on gradient imaging (Figure 5).
 
 During the 17-year follow-up period, the patient exhibited no symptoms. Biennially, MRI heads revealed no change in the lesion size or associated mass effect.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old male patient with a history of alcoholism and smoking was referred to the otorhinolaryngology department for dysphagia. Clinical examination revealed a painless 4 cm thickening lesion of the uvula. Cervical palpation revealed no adenopathy. A biopsy of the lesion was performed.
 Imaging Findings: CT scan revealed the presence of a uvula tumour (Figure 1), a suspicious hepatic lesion and a solid cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas. MRI showed a pancreatic body lesion, measuring 22 mm, with cystic and tissue components, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 2), and a 55 mm heterogeneous hepatic mass, of the segment IV, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 3). There was no dilatation of the pancreatic or biliary ducts. We performed an FDG PET-CT, which demonstrated an intensely hypermetabolic uvula (SUVmax at 9.2), with moderate hypermetabolism of the adenopathies (SUVmax at 2.5) and the liver lesion (SUVmax at 5.4) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old female patient presented at the Emergency Department with severe acute abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She also referred cessation of flatus and bowel movements for 4 days. Past medical history is unremarkable. On physical exam, the abdomen was distended and tender with no rebound or guarding.
 Imaging Findings: A plain erect abdominal radiograph revealed the presence of an air-distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant consistent with the inverted cecum, and multiple fluid levels (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) was later performed, with coronal and sagittal reconstructions (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5). On CT images, there was a markedly distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant with air-fluid level, measuring a maximum calliper of 7.5 cm, adjacent to a small blind-ending tubular structure compatible with the ileocecal appendix, consistent with the inverted cecum. A clockwise whirl sign composed of spiralled loops of collapsed cecum and ileum, with twisting of central fatty mesentery with enhancing distended vessels, was present (Figures 4a and 4b). The gradual tapering of the obstructed cecum at the site of the whirl resulted in a stenosis, with the appearance of a bird’s beak (Figure 5). There was also dilation of small bowel loops, filled with fluid. There were no signs of bowel perforation, as well as no signs of pneumatosis intestinalis or other signs of vascular compromise.

- "Bird's beak" tapering at obstruction site.  
- Small bowel dilation with fluid.  
- No perforation, no pneumatosis intestinalis, no vascular compromise.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Sigmoid volvulus  
   - Pros: Can show distended bowel loop and whirl sign.  
   - Cons: Typically involves sigmoid colon in the pelvis; not typically left upper quadrant cecal location; no inverted cecum; presence of inverted cecum points away.

2) Cecal volvulus  
   - Pros: Classic inverted cecum in left upper quadrant; whirl sign with collapsed cecum and ileum; bird's beak tapering at site of twist; obstruction predominantly of large bowel; no signs of perforation.  
   - Cons: None apparent; cecal volvulus fits location and imaging described.

3) Small bowel/large bowel mechanical obstruction  
   - Pros: General obstruction signs, dilated loops, fluid levels.  
   - Cons: Imaging is typical for cecal volvulus (inverted cecum, whirl sign, bird's beak); not a generic mechanical obstruction.

4) Intussusception  
   - Pros: Might show layered bowel loops.  
   - Cons: Absence of concentric rings or target sign typical for intussusception; location and findings better explained by volvulus; no mention of telescoping intestine loops.

5) Paralytic ileus  
   - Pros: Distended loops and fluid levels possible.  
   - Cons: No whirl sign or twisted mesentery; no inverted cecum; volvulus is mechanical obstruction, not functional; ileus typically shows uniformly dilated loops without site-specific obstruction.

C) Ranking:  
1) Cecal volvulus – matches all specific features: inverted cecum in left upper quadrant, whirl sign (clockwise), bird's beak tapering, obstruction site, no perforation.  
2) Sigmoid volvulus – whirl sign possible but location and inverted cecum argue against.  
3) Small bowel/large bowel mechanical obstruction – too generic, lacks site-specific volvulus signs.  
4) Intussusception – imaging signs absent.  
5) Paralytic ileus – inconsistent with whirl and inverted cecum findings.  
Thus, cecal volvulus is the diagnosis that best fits the imaging and clinical presentation.

</analysis><final> Cecal volvulus </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Our patient is a 64-year-old male admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to a prolonged cardiac arrest, who was on mechanical circulatory support with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). He was COVID-19-negative and presented with an encephalopathic syndrome.
 Imaging Findings: A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed, and the susceptibility-weighted imaging sequence (SWI), revealed multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs). These lesions were diffusely distributed across the subcortical white matter, splenium of the corpus callosum and posterior fossa, affecting the brainstem (midbrain and pons), as well as both cerebellar hemispheres. The cortex and periventricular regions were spared (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). In addition, we found small watershed infarctions (Figures 2a and 2b).

- Pros: Hypertensive encephalopathy can cause microbleeds, often deep gray nuclei (basal ganglia, thalami).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram)
 
 : Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein (Figure 3a), superficial and common femoral veins (Figure 3b) with heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels (Figure 3c).
 
 CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb)
 
 : A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion (Figure 4a) in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein (Figure 4b), superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins (Figure 4c).
 
 CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram)
 
 : Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries (Figure 1a), extending to the right interlobar artery (Figure 1b), right basal trunk (Figure 1c), and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments (Figure 2).
 
 PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography)
 
 : High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion (Figure 5b) and in the deep venous tumour thrombus (Figure 5a), bilateral pulmonary arteries (Figure 6b), right atrium (Figure 6a) and ventricle.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram): Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein, superficial and common femoral veins with a heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels. CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb): A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein, superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins. CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram): Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries, extending to the right interlobar artery, right basal trunk, and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments. PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography): High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion and in the deep venous tumour thrombus, bilateral pulmonary arteries, right atrium and ventricle.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma, Slow flow vascular malformation, Leiomyosarcoma, Vascular leiomyomatosis, Tumour thrombus, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Young female patient with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain.
- Diagnosis of extensive deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving left popliteal, superficial and common femoral veins.
- Imaging (USG, CTLL angiogram) reveals a lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion around popliteal vessels, intramuscular and intermuscular, extending into deep venous system (popliteal, superficial femoral, common femoral, and common iliac veins).
- CTPA shows extensive pulmonary artery involvement with thrombus, as well as thrombus in right atrium and ventricle.
- Multiple cavitary consolidations in bilateral lower lobes.
- PET-CT shows high-grade metabolic activity in the left thigh lesion and venous tumour thrombus, as well as in pulmonary arteries and cardiac chambers.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma
   - Pros: Rare primary intravascular sarcoma can present as intravascular mass with tumour thrombus.
   - Cons: Rare entity, patient age not typical (synovial sarcoma usually in young adults but intravascular primary synonym rare). No mention of synovial sarcoma characteristics (e.g., slow growth, typical sites). Also, synovial sarcoma usually soft tissue mass not intravascular.
2) Slow flow vascular malformation
   - Pros: Initial possibility of vascular malformation near popliteal vessels (as per clinical history).
   - Cons: Vascular malformations do not enhance heterogeneously nor produce high-grade metabolic activity on PET. Also, vascular malformations do not cause tumour thrombus or emboli; they are usually compressible venostructures, not solid enhancing masses.
3) Leiomyosarcoma
   - Pros: Tumour thrombus commonly seen; leiomyosarcoma can involve vessel walls; high metabolic activity consistent with malignancy.
   - Cons: Usually arises in larger veins like IVC or pulmonary vein, but can involve lower limb veins. The location in popliteal vein is unusual but not impossible. The lesion is extensive, intravascular, with tumour thrombus, consistent with leiomyosarcoma.
4) Vascular leiomyomatosis
   - Pros: Benign smooth muscle tumour that involves veins, can extend intravascularly and into right heart or pulmonary arteries. Typically occurs in women with prior uterine leiomyomas or hysterectomy, but can be primary. Can cause tumour thrombus, often extending contiguously from uterine veins, but also reported in other veins. High metabolic activity fits malignant or aggressive benign tumours.
   - Cons: Usually described as benign but with intravascular growth, can cause symptoms via obstruction. Additionally, ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis were observed (Figure 2).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old patient is referred from the primary care due to epigastric pain radiating to the left flank, accompanied by asthenia and episodes of postprandial nausea, as well as signs of panniculitis in both lower limbs. Blood tests revealed elevated CA 19-9 levels (135.5 U/mL).
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan was performed, revealing a hypodense lesion with cystic appearance in contact with the anteroinferior margin of the pancreatic tail, with areas of higher density inside it, which are suggestive of septations (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 A contrast-enhanced MRI was conducted to further characterise the lesion. It showed a heterogeneous tumour with an atypical cystic appearance, related to higher signal intensity on T1-weighted and lower intensity on T2-weighted images, when compared to free water and the presence of small cystic rounded areas with higher intensity on T2-weighted images (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Some small foci of signal drop on out-of-phase T1-weighted sequences compared with in-phase T1-weighted sequences were observed inside the lesion (Figures 3a and 3b). Additionally, areas of diffusion restriction were noted within the lesion (Figures 3c and 3d) and contrast-enhancing septations (Figure 3e). There was no visible communication with the main pancreatic duct.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old male with progressive imbalance while walking and generalised tonic-clonic seizures from 5 years of age. No significant family history. Neurological examination revealed lower limb spasticity, brisk reflexes, extensor plantar reflex, features of ataxia, impaired pain, and touch sensations. Ophthalmic and fundus examinations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed the atrophy of the superior cerebellar vermis and the atrophy of the superior cerebellar cortex. The inferior vermis and the inferior cerebellar cortex are spared (Figures 2 and 3). T2/FLAIR linear hypointense stripes in the pons at the level of superior and middle cerebellar peduncle giving a tigroid/striped appearance (Figures 1a and 1b). Atrophy of the cervical and thoracic spinal cord is seen (Figure 3). Mildly atrophic pre-central and post-central gyri (Figure 4) with the thinned-out body of corpus callosum (Figure 2) also seen.

- Ophthalmic and fundus exams normal (suggests no optic atrophy or retinal degeneration).
- MRI brain: atrophy of superior cerebellar vermis and superior cerebellar cortex with sparing of inferior vermis and cerebellar cortex.
- T2/FLAIR: linear hypointense stripes in the pons at the level of superior and middle cerebellar peduncles with a striped or "tigroid" appearance.
- Atrophy of cervical and thoracic spinal cord.
- Mild atrophy of precentral and postcentral gyri and thin corpus callosum.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix–Saguenay (ARSACS)
   - Pros:
     - Age at onset often in early childhood or first decade; here seizure onset at 5 years matches early onset.
     - Clinical features include spasticity and ataxia.
     - MRI hallmark: superior cerebellar vermis and superior cerebellar cortex atrophy, with sparing of inferior parts; this exact distribution is characteristic.
     - Linear T2 hypointensity stripes in the pons at superior and middle cerebellar peduncles giving a striped appearance is pathognomonic for ARSACS.
     - Spinal cord atrophy also reported in ARSACS.
   - Cons:
     - No mentioned optic atrophy or retinal pathology, which can be present but is variable.
     - No explicit mention of peripheral neuropathy, which may be seen in ARSACS but is not essential for diagnosis here.

2) Friedreich’s ataxia
   - Pros:
     - General neurodegeneration with ataxia and spasticity.
     - Usually presents in adolescence.
   - Cons:
     - MRI hallmark differs: Friedreich’s ataxia tends to affect the spinal cord and dorsal root ganglia, but cerebellar atrophy pattern is not typically restricted to the superior vermis; no characteristic striped pontine hypointensity.
     - Onset here is earlier; Friedreich’s usually second decade.
     - Seizures are rare in Friedreich’s ataxia.
     - No mention of cardiomyopathy or diabetes, common in Friedreich’s.

   - Pros:
     - Causes progressive ataxia and neurodegeneration.
   - Cons:
     - MRI findings are usually less specific, often show cerebellar atrophy but not the stripe sign in the pons.
     - The T2 stripe in the pons is not typical.
     - Seizures are uncommon.
     - Age of onset not usually as early as 5 years.

1) Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix–Saguenay (ARSACS) – excellent match to MRI (superior cerebellar vermis/cortex atrophy, pontine striped hypointensity) and clinical picture.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old woman with a history of controlled asthma and splenectomy following a gunshot wound 30 years prior presented with a dry cough, occasionally producing yellow-green sputum. She reported intermittent moderate dyspnoea and wheezing on auscultation, with symptoms gradually worsening over the past 30 days.
 Imaging Findings: Chest computed tomography (CCT) revealed solid nodules with homogeneous attenuation and enhancement (Figure 1), smooth borders, no calcifications (Figure 2), and no distortion of the adjacent lung parenchyma within the left pleural space (Figure 3). The nodules were randomly distributed along the longitudinal axis and measured up to 5.9 cm at the base of the hemithorax (Figure 4). A previous splenectomy was confirmed by the absence of the spleen in the abdominal cavity (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 76-year-old male patient with a prior diagnosis of cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) was referred to the emergency department from a nursing home due to progressive clinical deterioration over the past 24 hours. The patient presented decreased consciousness. No fever or recent trauma was reported. An urgent CT scan was requested (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT performed four years earlier at the onset of CAA (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed an isolated convexity subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) in the right frontal sulcus without trauma, in a patient with mild cognitive impairment.
 
 In the MRI one month after SAH, susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) sequence (Figure 2), significant superficial haemosiderosis and multiple supra and infratentorial microhaemorrhages are observed as hypointense areas with a “blooming” effect.
 
 Urgent CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 4) demonstrated an intraparenchymal haematoma in the right basal ganglia, a smaller left frontal haemorrhage, and extensive hypodensity in the left supratentorial white matter suggestive of cerebral oedema.
 
 In the MRI, two weeks after the acute event and corticosteroid treatment, hyperintensities in white matter on T2-weighted (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c) and FLAIR (Figures 5d and 5e) sequences are observed, without diffusion restriction (Figures 5f and 5g), corresponding to vasogenic oedema with improvement compared to the previous CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 
 CT one month after the acute event (Figures 6a, 6b, 6c, 6d and 7) showed near-complete resolution of cerebral oedema, confirming the response to corticosteroid treatment.

In the MRI, two weeks after the acute event and corticosteroid treatment, hyperintensities in white matter on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences are observed, without diffusion restriction, corresponding to vasogenic oedema with improvement compared to the previous CT. CT one month after the acute event showed near-complete resolution of cerebral oedema, confirming the response to corticosteroid treatment.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 68-year-old male, a known case of chronic kidney disease (CKD) stage V on dialysis, complaints of swelling in the left forearm and difficulty in cannulation. The patient had a history of left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula formation with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) brachiocephalic graft. Local examination showed dilated tortuous swellings on the left forearm reaching up to the cubital fossa.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination showed a relatively well-defined, predominantly hypoechoic lesion with a cystic component within, showing multiple septations and communication to the underlying vessel.
 
 Colour Doppler examination showed a typical “to-and-fro” pattern of flow (yin-yang sign) within the lesion and vessel with turbulent flow adjacent to the lesion. Communication with flow was noted between the lesion and the adjacent vessel.

1) True aneurysm:
   - Pro: "Well-defined lesion" with dilated vessel involvement.
   - Con: Typically presents as a focal dilatation of vessel wall without cystic components or multiple septations; "to-and-fro" flow more typical for pseudoaneurysm.

2) Anastomotic pseudoaneurysm (PTFE brachiocephalic bypass graft):
   - Pro: Communication with vessel, "to-and-fro" flow pattern, cystic lesion with septations, location near dialysis graft site.
   - Con: None significant; matches clinical and imaging findings well.

3) Haematoma:
   - Pro: Hypoechoic lesion possible.
   - Con: No mention of acute trauma; no typical echogenic layering or no flow pattern; "to-and-fro" flow would not be seen.

4) Arteriovenous malformation:
   - Pro: Abnormal vascular channel found.
   - Con: Usually multiple tortuous vessels, complex nidus; "to-and-fro" flow specific for pseudoaneurysm; no mention of nidus or tangle of vessels.

   - Pro: Can present as aneurysm-like dilatations.

6) Abscess:

2) True aneurysm - similar but lacks typical pseudoaneurysm flow pattern and cystic features.
3) Arteriovenous malformation - less likely due to lack of nidus and typical flow pattern.
4) Mycotic aneurysm - no infection signs.
5) Haematoma - no trauma, no typical echogenicity.

Anastomotic pseudoaneurysm (PTFE brachiocephalic bypass graft)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks of gestation presented with a growing, painless mass in her left breast. The patient had no significant past medical history or family history of breast cancer. Clinical examination revealed a firm, mobile, non-tender mass in the retro-areolar region of the left breast.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging at 12 weeks gestation showed a well-circumscribed lobulated heterogeneous mass with predominantly isoechoic echogenicity containing hyperechoic areas. No increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound was noted. These features could suggest a benign mass with lactational changes (e.g., fibroadenoma, hamartoma, or galactocele) (BI-RADS category 3) (Figures 1a and 1b). At 24 weeks, the lesion grew to 100×53×114 mm (Figures 2a and 2b). At 38 weeks of gestation, the mass was occupying the whole breast. The increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound is most likely due to breast engorgement during pregnancy (Figure 3). A core needle biopsy confirmed the lesion as benign with no evidence of malignancy (Figure 4).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 27-year-old pregnant woman, 12 weeks gestation.  
- Firm, mobile, non-tender, retro-areolar mass in left breast.  
- Ultrasound: well-circumscribed, lobulated, heterogeneous, mostly isoechoic with hyperechoic areas.  
- No increased vascularity early, later increased vascularity likely from breast engorgement.  
- Mass grew substantially from 12 to 38 weeks, occupying whole breast by term.  
- Core needle biopsy confirmed benign lesion, no malignancy.  
- Clinical and imaging features suggest benign lesion with lactational changes, but significant growth and size raise suspicion for potentially more aggressive or rare benign entities.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Fibroadenoma  
   - Pros: Common benign breast mass in young women, firm and mobile, retro-areolar location common.  
   - Cons: Typically does not enlarge dramatically to occupy whole breast; usually well-circumscribed and homogeneous; biopsy would be benign, consistent; however, fibroadenomas can enlarge during pregnancy.  
2) Galactocele  
   - Pros: Benign lactational lesion, common during pregnancy/lactation, usually well-circumscribed with isoechoic and hyperechoic areas (due to milk content).  
   - Cons: Typically cystic or partially cystic with low vascularity; significant growth to occupy whole breast is uncommon.  
3) Phyllodes tumour  
   - Pros: Can enlarge rapidly, large size, often in women around 40 but also younger; can be benign, borderline, or malignant.  
   - Cons: Usually shows more vascularity, leaf-like pattern on imaging, sometimes increased vascularity early; biopsy initially benign - possible, but phyllodes often show stromal overgrowth and increased vascularity.  
4) Leiomyosarcoma  
   - Cons: Malignant, rare breast tumor; would show irregular borders, heterogeneous echotexture, and increased vascularity early; biopsy did not show malignancy.  
5) Adenomyoepithelioma  
   - Pros: Benign or borderline tumor of biphasic breast tissue; can present as well-circumscribed mass.  
   - Cons: Rare, typically small, not associated with rapid growth to occupying whole breast; histology would likely show biphasic cells; biopsy would note this.  
6) Myoid hamartoma of the breast (MHB)  
   - Pros: Benign lesion with myoid (smooth muscle) elements, can be large and well-circumscribed; can contain heterogeneous echogenicity; biopsy would confirm benign smooth muscle cells; can be asymptomatic or present as a mass.  
   - Cons: Rare; but fits with benign biopsy and large size; no vascularity early could correspond to its fibrous content; increased vascularity later due to pregnancy changes.  
7) Breast carcinoma  
   - Cons: Malignant, usually irregular, spiculated margins; biopsy would detect malignancy; vascularity may be increased earlier; not consistent with benign biopsy.

C) Ranking:  
1) Myoid hamartoma of the breast (MHB) - best fits large, well-circumscribed, heterogeneous benign lesion with benign biopsy and significant growth due to pregnancy, without early increased vascularity.  
2) Fibroadenoma - common benign lesion; can enlarge in pregnancy; but less likely to occupy whole breast and less heterogeneous than described.  
3) Phyllodes tumour - possible due to growth, but imaging features and biopsy didn't strongly suggest stromal overgrowth or vascularity early.  
4) Galactocele - possible due to lactational changes and isoechoic/hyperechoic areas, but size and vascularity less typical.  
5) Adenomyoepithelioma - rare, but less likely given size and growth pattern.  
6) Leiomyosarcoma - excluded by benign biopsy.  
7) Breast carcinoma - excluded by benign biopsy and well-circumscribed margins.

<final>  
Myoid hamartoma of the breast (MHB)  
</final><|return|>
options: Fibroadenoma | Galactocele | Phyllodes tumour | Leiomyosarcoma | Adenomyoepithelioma | Myoid hamartoma of the breast (MHB) | Breast carcinoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old male presents with a history of microscopic haematuria, a palpable right flank mass, and recurrent headaches. A CT scan was performed, followed by a brain MRI to rule out tumoural pathology. The patient had no significant medical history.
 Imaging Findings: In the abdominal CT scan, multiple bilateral renal cystic lesions with septum formation were observed. The right kidney had multiple, irregular, heterogeneous lesions with significant enhancement post-contrast, and hypodense central areas related to necrosis, the biggest located in the superior pole. Similar characteristics were noted in the left kidney lesions (Figure 1). Also, multiple simple pancreatic cysts and splenic vein dilation were observed, along with pancreaticosplenic collaterals. There is a lesion in the splenic hilum that enhances and follows contrast in different phases, suggestive of a splenic hamartoma (Figure 2).
 
 The brain MRI revealed multiple heterogeneous lesions with a cystic and a solid component in the posterior fossa, showing avid and homogeneous enhancement of the solid portion, and causing vasogenic oedema and mass effect on the pons (Figures 3a, 3b, and 4).

A) Key findings:

- Extra-renal cysts: pancreatic cysts, splenic hamartoma, splenic vein dilation, and pancreaticosplenic collaterals.  
- Brain MRI: multiple heterogeneous lesions with cystic and solid components in the posterior fossa, avid and homogeneous enhancement of solid portions, causing vasogenic edema and mass effect on the pons.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Sporadic haemangioblastomas  
- Pros: Brain lesion morphology (solid and cystic components with avid enhancement) fits haemangioblastoma description.  
- Cons: Multifocality with bilateral renal tumors, pancreatic cysts, and splenic hamartoma not typical for sporadic cases. Usually, sporadic cases do not have systemic multisystem involvement.  

2) Von HippelâLindau syndrome (VHL)  
- Pros: Multisystem involvement is classic: bilateral renal cysts and RCCs (irregular heterogeneous enhancing lesions with necrosis), multiple pancreatic cysts, and CNS lesions typical for haemangioblastomas in the posterior fossa. Splenic hamartoma and splenic vein dilation/pancreaticosplenic collaterals can be associated. The age and gender fit the common presentation.   
- Cons: None significant; findings are quite textbook for VHL.

- Pros: Pancreatic cysts can be seen in MEN1.  
- Cons: No mention of pancreatic endocrine tumors, pituitary adenomas, or parathyroid disease. The central nervous system lesions and renal masses do not fit MEN1.  

4) Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC)  
- Pros: Renal cysts possible; occasional brain lesions, cortical tubers, or subependymal giant cell astrocytomas.  
- Cons: No calcified subependymal nodules or cortical tubers mentioned; brain lesions described are haemangioblastoma-like, not astrocytomas or tubers. Pancreatic cysts and splenic hamartoma are not typical.  

5) Polycystic kidney disease (PKD)  
- Pros: Bilateral renal cysts.  
- Cons: No family history or other cysts in liver or pancreas mentioned (though there are pancreatic cysts). Brain lesions and splenic hamartoma not part of PKD. Also, the renal lesions are suspicious for RCCs, not just simple cysts.  

6) Hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC)  
- Pros: RCC in both kidneys.  
- Cons: No description of cutaneous or uterine leiomyomas (patient male, so uterine leiomyomas out). No CNS lesions typical for HLRCC. Pancreatic cysts and splenic hamartoma not typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Von HippelâLindau syndrome (VHL) - most fitting, pathognomonic multisystem involvement (renal RCCs, pancreatic cysts, brain haemangioblastomas, splenic hamartoma).  
2) Sporadic haemangioblastomas - fits brain lesions but not systemic findings.  
3) Tuberous sclerosis complex - brain lesions possible, but not matching described lesions.  
4) HLRCC - renal tumors but missing other features.  
5) MEN 1 - mismatch with CNS and kidney findings.  
6) Polycystic kidney disease - renal cysts alone insufficient, systemic findings argue against.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old woman presented to an otorhinolaryngology consultation with a 2-month history of dysphonia and a 2-year history of gradual right hearing loss. No other symptoms were reported. Right vocal cord paralysis was noted at flexible nasofibrolaryngoscopy. A CT of the neck and chest was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT demonstrated signs of right vocal cord paralysis, right tongue haemiatrophy, and atrophy of the right trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles (Figure 1). A lytic lesion in the right skull base was noted, only partially covered, given the undirected protocol. There were no other relevant findings.
 
 A brain and skull base CT and MRI were requested for further evaluation. CT showed a lytic lesion centred at the petroclival synchondrosis, with extensive destruction of the clivus and petrous temporal bone, showing calcified areas and involvement of the foramen jugular and carotid canal (Figure 2).
 
 At MRI, the lesion showed marked T2WI hyperintensity, T1WI hypointensity, and heterogeneous gadolinium enhancement (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). There was no restriction to the diffusion of water molecules, with high ADC values (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 The lesion infiltrated the right cavernous sinus, right Meckel’s cave, foramen rotundum, foramen ovale, and the hypoglossal canal (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).

 Imaging Findings: CT demonstrated signs of right vocal cord paralysis, right tongue haemiatrophy, and atrophy of the right trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles. model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 24-year-old woman with right-sided vocal cord paralysis, right tongue hemiatrophy, and atrophy of the right trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles (suggesting damage to the lower cranial nerves VII, IX, X, XI, and XII).  
- Lytic lesion in the right skull base, centered at the petroclival synchondrosis with extensive destruction of the clivus and petrous temporal bone.  
- Involvement of foramen jugular, carotid canal, cavernous sinus, Meckel's cave, foramen rotundum, foramen ovale, and hypoglossal canal (multiple cranial nerve foramina).  
- Imaging characteristics: T2 hyperintensity, T1 hypointensity, heterogeneous gadolinium enhancement, no diffusion restriction (high ADC values).  
- Calcified areas present within the lesion.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Skull base chordoma  
   - Pros: Commonly arises at clival region; typically destructive and lytic; T2 hyperintensity; often contains calcifications.  
   - Cons: Usually midline or slightly off-center, but can involve asymmetrically; less commonly causes extensive foraminal involvement; usually does not cause multiple lower cranial nerve palsies symmetrically.  

2) Skull base chondrosarcoma  
   - Pros: Usually arises lateral to midline (petrous apex, foramen lacerum); may contain calcifications; T2 hyperintensity; destructive; can infiltrate multiple foramina.  
   - Cons: Age slightly older typically; but young adult possible. Does not strongly show lower cranial nerve palsies causing tongue and shoulder muscle atrophy as commonly; but can invade multiple nerve pathways.  

3) Skull base paraganglioma  
   - Pros: Can involve petrous apex and cavernous sinus region; commonly hypervascular tumors with intense enhancement.  
   - Cons: Typically don't cause bone destruction or lytic lesions; usually enhance strongly, not lytic with calcifications; usually don't cause progressive extensive bone destruction or lower cranial nerve palsies due to mass effect; usually highly vascular.  

4) Giant cell tumour of the skull base  
   - Pros: Can be lytic and locally aggressive.  
   - Cons: Rare in skull base; usually middle-aged adults; rarely presents with extensive foraminal involvement and nerve palsies. Growth pattern more localized; not typically involving multiple foramina and causing multi-nerve palsies.  

5) Langerhans cell histiocytosis of the skull base  
   - Pros: Lytic lesions with possible bone destruction; children/young adults; can involve multiple skull base regions.  
   - Cons: Usually less extensive bone destruction in this area; often not associated with widespread foraminal involvement and cranial nerve palsies, although possible; usually no extensive calcifications.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Skull base chordoma: Most likely given lesion centered at petroclival synchondrosis, T2 hyperintensity, lytic and destructive lesion with calcifications and involvement of multiple foramina consistent with slow-growing skull base tumor. The extensive foraminal involvement and nerve palsies also fit.  
2) Skull base chondrosarcoma: Similar imaging features but usually lateral to midline and less commonly cause extensive nerve palsies; however, not excluded.  
3) Langerhans cell histiocytosis: Possible but less likely given extent and soft tissue involvement.  
4) Giant cell tumour: Unlikely due to rarity at this site and patient age.  
5) Paraganglioma: Least likely due to lytic nature, calcifications, and extensive bone destruction.  

<final>Skull base chordoma</final><|return|>
options: Skull base chordoma | Skull base chondrosarcoma | Skull base paraganglioma | Giant cell tumour of the skull base | Langerhans cell histiocytosis of the skull base

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old male driver presented with insidious onset pain and effusion of the right knee for 3 weeks. The patient had a similar history the previous year, which resolved after taking nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). The knee range of motion was terminally painful. There was no history of trauma, arthritis or comorbidities.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the right knee revealed normal bone density without fracture or erosion. MRI of the right knee revealed patchy areas in the epimetaphyseal region of both the femoral condyles, showing hyperintense signals on proton density fat-suppressed (PDFS) images (Figure 1) and correlating hypointense signals on T1W images (Figure 2), suggestive of marrow oedema. Mild knee joint effusion was noted without synovial thickening, erosions or low signal lines. The ligaments and menisci were normal. The tibial condyles, fibula and patella showed normal marrow signal intensity. The articular cartilage, posteromedial and posterolateral corners of the knee joint were normal.

A) Key findings: 
- 42-year-old male driver with insidious knee pain and effusion over 3 weeks.
- Similar episode the previous year, resolved with NSAIDs.
- Physical exam: terminally painful range of motion.
- No trauma, arthritis, or comorbidities.
- Radiographs normal bone density, no fracture or erosion.
- MRI: patchy epimetaphyseal marrow edema in both femoral condyles (hyperintense on PDFS, hypointense on T1W), mild joint effusion, no synovial thickening or erosions, normal ligaments, menisci, other bones, and cartilage.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Osteonecrosis:
   - Pros: marrow edema in epimetaphyseal region could be early osteonecrosis.
   - Cons: biphasic marrow edema affecting both femoral condyles is atypical; osteonecrosis usually shows subchondral hypo-intensity or a double-line sign on T2/STIR not described; no fracture or subchondral collapse; previous episode resolved without sequelae, less common for osteonecrosis; usually subchondral and more focal.
2) Inflammatory arthritis:
   - Pros: joint effusion.
   - Cons: no synovial thickening, erosions, or bone destruction; patient has no known arthritis; marrow edema patterns are not typical for inflammatory arthritis which commonly involves synovium and cartilage.

   - Pros: recurrent pain, effusion.
   - Cons: no evidence of soft tissue edema, skin changes, or bone changes typical of CRPS; CRPS often involves soft tissue swelling and heterogeneous marrow signal, not just isolated patchy marrow edema.
4) Bone marrow oedema syndrome:
   - Pros: classic presentation: young adult, insidious knee pain with marrow edema on MRI, normal radiograph; recurrent episodes are possible; no erosions or synovial thickening; marrow edema patchy and involves epimetaphyseal region of femoral condyles.
   - Cons: none significant here; findings match this entity.
5) Stress fracture:
   - Pros: marrow edema in epimetaphyseal region.
   - Cons: no fracture line, no cortical disruption or edema in cortex; stress fracture usually shows linear low signal on T1/T2; radiographs normal; no trauma history.

C) Ranking:
1) Bone marrow oedema syndrome (BMOS) - best fit: classic clinical and imaging findings, typical location and MRI signal, recurrent episodes, normal radiographs.
2) Osteonecrosis - less likely due to bilateral involvement and lack of subchondral changes.
3) Complex regional pain syndrome - clinical history less typical, no soft tissue changes.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old male presented to the paediatric department with a history of headache, which was severe and diffuse in nature and associated with 2–3 episodes of non-projectile vomiting. There was no associated history of blurring of vision, loss of consciousness, seizure, trauma, fever, pain abdomen or loose stool. On examination, he had an emaciated appearance with microcephaly and hyperextensible joints.
 
 The patient had developmental delay since infancy, which he gradually gained at >3 years of age. He had an episode of seizure at 6 years of age, and his EEG revealed generalised epileptiform discharges. He was started on valproate, which was stopped in 2020. He continued to have multiple episodes of headache and vomiting during this period. He presented with bilateral papilledema in September 2021.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed T2 hyperintensities (Figures 1a and 1b) involving bilateral subcortical, deep, periventricular and cerebellar white matter with sparing of the perirolandic white matter. These areas show restriction of diffusion (Figures 2a and 2b) and do not show blooming on gradient echo (GRE) images. There is a prominence of bilateral optic nerves with tortuosity and optic nerve sheath dilatation (Figures 3a and 3b) with mild subluxation of both lenses (Figure 4). There is a relative increase in choline creatinine peak with preserved N-acetylaspartate (NAA) in TE 135 ms (Figure 5).
 
 Imaging done 3 months later revealed a mild reduction in global white matter hyperintensity (Figure 6).
 
 The next imaging, done 2 years later, revealed a significant reduction in the white matter hyperintensities and restriction of diffusion involving the white matter (Figure 7).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with 2 days of severe right-sided lower abdominal pain. He was pyrexial with a raised neutrophil count (9.2x10^9/L) and CRP (55mg/L). Examination revealed right inguinal swelling and tenderness. Due to the high suspicion of incarcerated inguinal hernia, a CT abdomen and pelvis was requested.
 Imaging Findings: A portal venous phase CT abdomen and pelvis found a thickened and hyper-enhancing right spermatic cord, with the supra-scrotal vas deferens measuring up to 5mm. Further inflammation was seen within the inguinal canal, and a small reactive hydrocele was noted. A unilaterally enlarged right seminal vesicle was identified, however, there was no abscess.
 
 On the assumption that these findings were due to infection, oral ciprofloxacin was commenced, with temperatures and pain settling within the first 24 hours. The patient has not had any subsequent presentations. A right inguinal hernia repair was also performed during the inpatient stay with antibiotic cover to reduce the risk of infection. There were no complications.

- No abscess noted.
- Clinical picture improved rapidly with antibiotics, with complete resolution and no recurrence.
- Underwent right inguinal hernia repair without complications.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Acute vasitis:
   - Pros: Inflammation of the spermatic cord (thickened, hyper-enhancing), enlarged vas deferens, inflammation in inguinal canal, clinical presentation with pain, swelling, and infection signs. Rapid response to antibiotics supports an inflammatory/infectious cause.
   - Cons: May mimic hernia or epididymo-orchitis but CT findings are more specific for vasitis.

2) Incarcerated inguinal hernia:
   - Pros: Clinical suspicion warranted CT; inguinal swelling and pain.
   - Cons: CT findings do not show bowel or other herniated contents, no obstruction, no strangulation; rather, the imaging shows inflammatory findings in cord and vas deferens.    - Pros: Inguinal hernia could include appendix.
   - Cons: Imaging does not show appendix within inguinal canal, no abscess or signs of appendiceal inflammation; imaging findings are localized to spermatic cord and inguinal canal inflammation rather than appendix. No mention of appendiceal involvement.

C) Ranking:
1) Acute vasitis (most likely): The combination of spermatic cord thickening and hyperenhancement, enlarged vas deferens, inguinal canal inflammation, quick response to antibiotics, and no hernia findings on CT strongly support acute vasitis.
2) Incarcerated inguinal hernia: Clinical suspicion led to imaging, but no hernia contents identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 51-year-old male patient presented with right-sided limb weakness and slurred speech for a month. No vomiting/seizures/syncope was reported.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a well-defined lesion in the left periventricular region, measuring 1.8 x 1.5 x 1.6 cm (AP x ML x CC) with significant perifocal oedema (Figure 1). The lesion was T2 hetero-intense (Figure 1) with a T2 hypointense and T1 hyperintense rim (Figure 2), showing diffusion restriction (Figure 4). Associated mass effects noted were midline shift to the right, left uncal herniation, and effacement of the left lateral ventricle (Figure 6). Extensive vasogenic oedema was noted involving the left gangliocapsular region, left frontotemporal lobe, left hemi-midbrain, and left hemi-pons (Figure 1). On post-contrast images, the lesion showed heterogenous enhancement with involvement of the left lateral ventricular margin (Figure 3).
 
 Tractography showed interruption of white matter tracts in the region of the lesion (Figure 5a). The region of perilesional oedema showed only deviation of the left internal capsule and left superior longitudinal fasciculus tracts (Figure 5a), explaining the patient’s symptoms. Fractional anisotropy was decreased (Figure 5b) while mean diffusivity was increased (Figure 5c) within the lesion and in the region of perilesional oedema.
 
 The patient then underwent left FP craniotomy and excision of the lesion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman came to the emergency department with complaints of multiple bone pains. She reported fractures of the right forearm and the right leg three years ago, occurring without any significant trauma. Laboratory findings revealed elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP > 1400) and parathyroid hormone (720 pg/ml), serum calcium level was 10.8 mg/dl, and serum creatinine level was 0.9. There was no skin lesion.
 Imaging Findings: Skeletal Survey (Radiograph of the skull, dorso-lumbar spine and both upper and lower extremities)
 
 The shafts of the long bones showed ground glass opacities with expansion of the medullary cavity and thinning of the cortex. There were multiple lytic lesions in the shaft of the long bones, and a few of them showed a sclerotic rim. There were areas of sclerosis in the metaphysis of the long bones (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). The dorsal lumbar spine showed reduced bone density with endplate sclerosis, consistent with a rugger-jersey spine (Figure 2b). Multiple lytic lesions were seen in the scapula, ribs, clavicle, multiple vertebral bodies, pelvic bones and metacarpals on both sides (Figures 2a and 2c). There was subperiosteal resorption on the radial aspect of the head of the second proximal phalanx (Figure 3). Skull radiograph showed a widening of the diploic spaces with multiple well-defined, variable-sized sclerotic lesions (Figure 4).
 
 CT Brain
 
 The CT brain revealed a widening of diploic spaces with multiple well-defined sclerotic and ground glass density lesions (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c).
 
 USG Neck
 
 In the US of the neck, there was a well-defined heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion posterior to the right lobe of the thyroid (Figure 6).
 
 CT Parathyroid Protocol
 
 A hypodense enhancing lesion posterior to the right lobe of the thyroid was detected, suggestive of parathyroid adenoma (Figures 7a, 7b, 8a, 8b, 9a, 9b, 9c).

A) Key findings:
- Young woman (20 years old) with multiple bone pains and history of fractures without trauma.
- Laboratory: Elevated alkaline phosphatase (>1400), elevated parathyroid hormone (720 pg/ml), normal serum calcium (10.8 mg/dl), normal creatinine (0.9).
- Radiographs: ground glass opacity and expansion of medullary cavity with cortical thinning in long bone shafts.
- Multiple lytic lesions with sclerotic rims in long bones, sclerosis in metaphyses, and "rugger-jersey spine" appearance (endplate sclerosis).
- Subperiosteal resorption on radial aspect of second proximal phalanx (classic for hyperparathyroidism).
- Skull: widening of diploic spaces with multiple variable-sized sclerotic lesions (ground glass).
- Neck ultrasound and CT parathyroid protocol reveal a hypodense enhancing lesion posterior to the right thyroid lobe (parathyroid adenoma).

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Primary hyperparathyroidism with fibrous dysplasia of bone
- Pros: Elevated PTH, subperiosteal resorption, rugger-jersey spine are classical for hyperparathyroidism; presence of parathyroid adenoma; multiple lytic lesions with sclerotic rims can be seen in fibrous dysplasia.
- Cons: Parathyroid adenoma confirmed; fibrous dysplasia usually presents as monostotic or polyostotic lesions with characteristic ground glass matrix; hyperparathyroidism typically does not cause ground glass matrix lesions, but it can cause lytic lesions and bone demineralization.

2) Paget’s disease of bone
- Pros: Ground glass opacities, cortical thickening, and expansion could suggest Paget’s disease.
- Cons: Paget's disease usually affects older adults (usually >55 years), not a 20-year-old woman; there is no mention of classic Paget's bone remodeling patterns like thickened cortices with coarsened trabeculae, and the presence of elevated PTH and parathyroid adenoma argue against Paget's disease. Also, rugger-jersey spine is more typical of hyperparathyroidism than Paget's disease.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
1) Primary hyperparathyroidism with fibrous dysplasia of bone
2) Paget’s disease of bone

Justification: The presence of elevated PTH, parathyroid adenoma, subperiosteal bone resorption, and rugger-jersey spine are pathognomonic for primary hyperparathyroidism. Additionally, the imaging findings of multiple lytic lesions with sclerotic rims and ground glass lesions can be explained by fibrous dysplasia, which can coexist with hyperparathyroidism as a separate bone pathology. Paget's disease is less likely given patient age and PTH findings.

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old woman presented with permanent pain of increasing intensity in her right leg that had been evolving for 24 hours. The pain appeared immediately following a horse ride in an improperly adjusted stirrup and was not relieved by paracetamol, ketoprofen and morphine. No significant medical history.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the legs showed a swollen appearance of the right anterolateral compartment muscles with:
 
 Fat-suppressed coronal T2-weighted (Figure 1) and axial PD-SPIR (Figure 2) emphasised the acute involvement of the lateral compartment of the leg through a noticeable bulging of the compartment muscles, which is clearly visible in the axial view when compared to the healthy contralateral side. Additional loss of fascicular architecture is seen. Diffuse hyperintensity of the compartment indicates muscular oedema.
 
 Sagittal T1-weighted sequence showed slight spontaneous high signal intensity within the involved muscles (Figure 3).
 
 After injection, the fat-suppressed axial T1-weighted sequence (Figure 4) showed focal defects of enhancement within the proximal portion of the muscles, indicating areas of ischemia.
 
 No other causes of acute pain, including a stress fracture, deep vein thrombosis or focal muscular strain, were identified on the examination. The diagnosis of acute compartment syndrome was confirmed by MRI.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old woman presents with a left preauricular lump of two weeks’ evolution. Physical examination reveals a soft, mobile 1 cm left preauricular lump upon superficial palpation. An MRI and a fine-needle aspiration biopsy was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Facial MRI revealed a small lesion in the superficial lobe of the left parotid gland, located cranial to the Stensen duct, and anterior and lateral to the facial nerve, showing mild heterogeneity and peripheral enhancement after contrast injection. It exhibited a very low apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) value on the ADC map (0.4 to 0.5x10^-3 mm
 
 2
 
 /second) and a type III TIC (time-intensity curve), suggesting radiological aggressiveness (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 1d, and 1e).
 
 Additionally, a marked enlargement of the left lacrimal gland was observed, with similar radiological findings on diffusion and dynamic gadolinium-enhancement sequences, indicative of an infiltrative process (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, and 2e).
 
 The low ADC values on both lesions gave the clue to diagnose a possible concomitant lymphoproliferative process, and this finding is key to the diagnosis [1].

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old female patient presented with complaints of fever, altered sensorium and convulsions with a short history of 2 days. Her vitals were normal. She was further advised for an MRI brain non-contrast study for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, the MRI brain non-contrast study was performed, and it revealed T2 hyperintense signal abnormality with FLAIR hyperintense signal changes involving bilateral medial temporal lobes. No abnormal post-contrast neuro-parenchymal or leptomeningeal enhancement was noted.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old female patient presented with complaints of fever, altered sensorium and convulsions with a short history of 2 days. Her vitals were normal. She was further advised for an MRI brain non-contrast study for further evaluation.

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Limbic encephalitis, Herpes simplex encephalitis, Status epilepticus
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 40-year-old female.  
- Clinical presentation: fever, altered sensorium, convulsions (short history of 2 days).  
- Imaging: MRI brain non-contrast study.  
- Findings: T2 hyperintense signal abnormality with FLAIR hyperintense signal changes in bilateral medial temporal lobes.  
- No abnormal post-contrast neuro-parenchymal or leptomeningeal enhancement noted.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Limbic encephalitis:  
   - Pros: Limbic encephalitis classically involves medial temporal lobe structures (hippocampus/amygdala), causing T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in these areas. Clinical presentation can include seizures, memory impairment, and altered mental status. Bilateral involvement is typical. No enhancement required, though it can be present.  
   - Cons: Limbic encephalitis tends to have a subacute to chronic course rather than a short (2 days) acute onset. However, the clinical history can be variable, and some can present acutely.  

2) Herpes simplex encephalitis:  
   - Pros: Classical involvement of bilateral (often asymmetric) medial temporal lobes with T2/FLAIR hyperintensities. Clinical presentation fits acute fever, altered sensorium, seizures. Usually shows enhancement but may be absent early on. The presentation and imaging findings are classic for HSV encephalitis.  
   - Cons: No abnormal post-contrast enhancement noted, but early imaging may not show enhancement; non-contrast only done.  

3) Status epilepticus:  
   - Pros: Seizures (convulsions), altered sensorium. Status epilepticus can cause T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in affected regions, often cortex and subcortical white matter, sometimes hippocampus.  
   - Cons: Typically more cortical/subcortical cortical involvement; isolated medial temporal horn involvement is less common. Usually, there is a history of prolonged seizures; we only have convulsions. No cortical edema or other seizure-related changes described. Bilateral medial temporal lobe involvement specifically is less typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Herpes simplex encephalitis (HSE) - pathognomonic involvement of bilateral medial temporal lobes, classic presentation (fever, seizures, altered sensorium), imaging fits best.  
2) Limbic encephalitis - similar findings but usually subacute; clinical presentation was acute and typical of HSE.  
3) Status epilepticus - less likely given specific medial temporal involvement and lack of cortical changes.  

Therefore, the best diagnosis is Herpes simplex encephalitis.  
</analysis>  
<final>Herpes simplex encephalitis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 60-year-old female presented to the casualty with an episode of loss of consciousness for a period of thirty minutes. After regaining consciousness, the patient lost power in all four limbs and was unable to walk without assistance.
 Imaging Findings: On NCCT (non-contrast computed tomography) brain, multiple small hypodense cystic lesions were noted, many showing scolex inside depicted by the hyperdense eccentric dot within and surrounding oedema (Figures 1a and 1b). A few of them were seen with calcifications inside, suggesting calcified scolices, thus describing the different stages of the disease. A sub-centimetric calcified lesion was seen in the intermuscular plane between the left semispinalis capitis and trapezius muscle (Figures 1a and 1b). On MRI correlation, multiple well-defined sub-centimetric hyperintense lesions were noted in bilateral cerebral hemispheres. Internal eccentric scolex was noted; however, no evidence of mass effect was seen (Figures 2a and 2b). On X-ray examination, multiple calcified lesions were seen in the intermuscular plane along bilateral proximal lower limbs, suggesting disseminated disease and rice grain calcifications (Figure 3). Further ultrasonographic evaluation depicted a non-compressible, hypoechoic cystic lesion with a central calcification in the gracilis muscle along the medial compartment of the thigh (Figure 4).

- Some cysts show calcifications inside (calcified scolices), indicating different stages of a disease.
- A sub-centimetric calcified lesion in intermuscular plane between left semispinalis capitis and trapezius muscle.

- X-ray shows multiple calcified lesions in intermuscular planes of bilateral proximal lower limbs (rice grain calcifications).
- Ultrasound shows non-compressible, hypoechoic cystic lesion with central calcification in gracilis muscle.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Neurocysticercosis with disseminated cysticercosis
   Pros: Presence of cystic lesions with scolex (pathognomonic for cysticercosis) in brain, muscle, and soft tissue; calcified scolices indicating different stages; rice grain calcifications typical of disseminated cysticercosis; muscle involvement seen on US and X-ray.
   Cons: None significant; findings are classic for this disease.

2) Cerebral metastasis
   Pros: Multiple brain lesions.
   Cons: No mass effect; presence of scolex and calcified cysts are not typical for metastases; scolex is pathognomonic for cysticercosis, not metastases; muscle/cutaneous calcifications not typical.

3) Pyogenic cerebral abscess
   Pros: Brain lesions with surrounding edema.
   Cons: No mass effect; scolex is not seen in abscesses; calcified cysts with scolex are not typical; muscle lesions and rice grain calcifications are not characteristic for abscesses.

4) Tuberculoma
   Pros: Brain lesions with central calcifications.
   Cons: No scolex; tuberculomas do not show scolex; muscle lesions and rice grain calcifications are not typical; scalloped margins and ring-enhancement not mentioned; scolex is not a feature.

5) Toxoplasmosis
   Pros: Brain lesions possible.
   Cons: Scolex not seen in toxoplasmosis; lesion patterns differ; muscle involvement uncommon; rice grain calcifications absent.

C) Ranking:
