OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A young male presents with ataxic gait, numbness in lower limbs, and difficulty in standing and walking. To evaluate further, MR brain and MR spine screening was done, which suggests compensated hydrocephalus. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunting was done to treat this. After VP shunting, he presents with headache, with symmetrical weakness in the upper limbs and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: Pre-shunt
 
 There is prominence of the midline cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) space in between the lower parts of the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres inferior to the vermis, displacing the vermis mildly upward and the communication to the 4th ventricle—this may represent a Blake’s pouch cyst, an incidental finding as seen in Figure 1. There is moderate dilatation of both supra and infratentorial ventricular systems without obvious periventricular ooze, i.e., the possibility of compensated hydrocephalus as seen in Figure 3. There is evidence of engorgement of the epidural venous plexus causing mass effect over the cord, as seen in Figures 4a and 4b.
 
 Post-shunting
 
 There is evidence of enlargement of the ventral epidural space showing enhancement in the cervical spine and proximal dorsal spine up to the D2 level, with extension cranially till the foramen magnum with prominent flow voids, likely engorged epidural veins within it, causing cord compression as seen in Figure 5. There is evidence of an enlarged pituitary gland (mid sagittal height of 12.7 mm) as seen in Figure 6. There is evidence of a round dural venous sinus as seen in Figure 7. Given these findings, the possibility of excessive CSF diversion needs consideration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old premenopausal woman presented with dysmenorrhea. Ultrasound (USG) revealed a bulky uterus with multiple fibroids. She subsequently underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy combined with a left salpingo-oophorectomy and right salpingectomy. Six months post-surgery, she presented with abdominal fullness and difficulty breathing.
 Imaging Findings: A chest CT scan revealed numerous parenchymal and subpleural nodules of varying sizes, randomly distributed in both lungs (Figures 1a and 1b). No intralesional calcifications noted.
 
 A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen identified a large, heterogeneously enhancing mass with both solid and cystic components in the left para-aortic region, the aortocaval and left external iliac regions, exerting a mass effect on nearby visceral structures without signs of direct invasion (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 A USG-guided Tru-cut biopsy was performed on the left para-aortic solid-cystic mass and sent for histopathological examination (Figure 3).
 
 On follow-up FDG-PET/CT, the retroperitoneal masses showed no significant fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (Figures 4a and 4b).

   - Pros: Sclerosing bone lesions can be seen.

6) Sickle cell disease
   - Pros: Causes bone changes, marrow hyperplasia, and expansion.

5) Sickle cell disease (no sclerosing lesions typical)

Given the classic imaging pattern of mixed sclerosing bone dysplasias and the clinical presentation, the best fit is Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old female patient was referred to the radiology department in view of a history of recurrent patellar dislocation and knee joint pain aggravated for the last 20 days. No history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: The trochlear groove appears shallow with a sulcal angle measuring 165 degrees (>150 degrees is considered abnormal) (Figure 1). The lateral trochlear inclination angle measures 9 degrees (<11 degrees is considered abnormal) (Figure 2). A trochlear facet asymmetry is seen with a ratio measuring 34% (<40% is considered abnormal) (Figure 3). The trochlear depth measures 2.5 mm (<3 mm is considered abnormal) (Figure 4).
 
 Proton density/Fat saturation (PD/FS) hyperintensity is seen in the medial patellar retinaculum with partial disruption of the fibres (<50% thickness), suggestive of a low-grade partial thickness tear. A mild lateral subluxation of the patella is seen. Grade IV chondromalacic changes (Outerbridge staging) are seen in the lateral articular surface of the patella at the patellofemoral joint space (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen. Physical examination revealed a flat and soft abdomen with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had complete imaging data, including CT, MR, and PET-CT. All imaging examinations showed a mass in segment VII/VIII of the liver.
 
 Unenhanced CT scan showed that the lesion was low-density, with a CT value of about 23 HU, linear calcification at the edge, and mild shrinkage of the liver capsule. After enhancement, the lesion did not enhance, and the surrounding liver parenchyma was mildly enhanced in the arterial phase (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The mass contains mixed low signal on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images and T2-weighted fat-suppressed images (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Coronal T2-weighted fat-suppressed images and coronal venous-phase T1-weighted fat-suppressed images showed that the intrahepatic bile duct beside the mass is dilated and connected to the lesion; the edge of the lesion and the adjacent bile duct showed mild enhancement (Figures 3a and 3b). Diffusion-weighted image (DWI, b = 800 mm2/s) showed a slightly higher signal (Figure 4a). The ADC map does not show restricted diffusion (Figure 4b). Similar to the CT-enhanced images, on T1-weighted enhanced arterial phase images, it can also be seen that the liver parenchyma around the lesion is slightly enhanced (Figure 4c).
 
 PET-CT showed a slight increase in FDG metabolism (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d).

A) Key findings:
- Age and gender: 53-year-old male.
- Clinical presentation: persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen, mild tenderness, flat and soft abdomen.
- Imaging: CT, MR, and PET-CT.
- Lesion location: liver segment VII/VIII.

- MRI: Mixed low T1 signal, hyperintense T2 signal, bile duct dilatation adjacent and connecting with lesion, mild enhancement of lesion edge and adjacent bile duct. Slightly high signal on DWI (b=800), no restricted diffusion on ADC map.

1) Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma
   Pros: usually presents with bile duct dilation and proximal atrophy, mass often hypovascular or peripheral enhancement pattern, linear capsular retraction, and sometimes calcification. Slightly increased FDG uptake typical. Site in hepatic dome segments common.
   Cons: Usually demonstrates some delayed enhancement on imaging and more prominent FDG uptake often; sometimes more aggressive features.

2) Primary hepatic adenosarcoma
   Pros: Rare liver tumor, mix of epithelial and mesenchymal components, may show mixed signal intensity on MRI; however data extremely scarce. 
   Cons: Very rare, no typical imaging features described. No typical bile duct dilation or adjacent enhancement description in literature. Calcifications uncommon. Usually presents as a solid-cystic mass.

3) Undifferentiated carcinoma (sarcomatoid carcinoma)
   Pros: May appear as a hypovascular mass with heterogeneous signal because of necrosis. Slight FDG uptake can be expected. Can involve bile ducts. Some areas might show peripheral enhancement.
   Cons: Usually larger, more aggressive, often shows more pronounced FDG uptake. Calcifications uncommon. Bile duct stricture rather than duct dilation connecting to mass.

4) Hepatic fibroadenoma
   Pros: Rare benign liver tumor, can show well-defined mass with T1 low and T2 high signal. 
   Cons: Usually does not cause bile duct dilation or connect to bile ducts. No typical calcifications. Enhancement pattern usually homogenous and progressive. FDG uptake minimal or absent. Bile duct changes and liver capsular retraction uncommon.

C) Ranking:
1) Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma: Best match due to bile duct dilation, capsular retraction, low-density non-enhancing mass, linear calcification, and mild FDG uptake.
2) Undifferentiated carcinoma: Possible but less fitting with mild FDG uptake and lack of aggressive features.
3) Primary hepatic adenosarcoma: Very rare, insufficient imaging characteristics matching.
4) Hepatic fibroadenoma: Least likely due to bile duct involvement and lack of typical features.

<final> Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male with anal swelling and bleeding of years of evolution. Physical examination revealed a mass of approximately 20 cm in perianal region, with several fistulous tracts covering anal and perianal area with purulent secretion.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT showed a voluminous low-density gluteal mass with areas of internal enhancement (Figures 1a and 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a multiloculated, cauliflower-like 21 x 16 x 11cm gluteal mass. It extended through both ischioanal fossae and fistulised to the anal canal with two posterior tracts (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lesion was hypointense with respect to the muscle in T1-weighted sequences and showed high signal intensity in T2, compatible with abundant myxoid-mucinous content. Contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed T1-weighted MR imaging showed linear and papillary mesh-like enhancing areas (Figure 2d).

   - Pros: Purulent discharge, fistulous tracts, abscess is common in perianal infections.
   - Cons: Very large mass (21 x 16 x 11 cm) unlikely for abscess; multiloculated, cauliflower-like appearance and myxoid/mucinous content are unusual for simple abscess; no mention of acute inflammatory signs; chronicity points against simple abscess.

   - Cons: Typically cancer can be more solid; however mucinous carcinoma can have myxoid/mucinous signal.

   - Pros: Congenital cystic lesion in presacral region, can become large; multiloculated.
   - Cons: Tailgut cysts are usually midline presacral, not perianal or gluteal; they rarely fistulise to anal canal; less likely to cause fistulous tracts extending to anal canal.

5) Dermoid cyst:
   - Pros: Common benign cystic lesion in sacral/gluteal region.
   - Cons: Usually more homogeneous, may contain fat; not likely to fistulise or cause such large lesions with fistulous tracks; no mention of fat or calcification.

C) Ranking:
1) Carcinoma arising from anal fistula - best congruence: chronic fistula, large mass, enhancing papillary mesh-like areas suggesting tumor; location and fistulous tracts typical.
2) Tailgut cyst - less likely given location and fistulation.
3) Perianal abscess - less likely due to size and chronicity.
4) Dermoid cyst - unlikely due to lack of fat.
5) Teratoma - least likely given age and clinical presentation.

Tie break: Pathognomonic presence of fistulous tracts with papillary enhancing areas in a large multiloculated lesion is most typical of carcinoma arising from chronic anal fistula.

</analysis>

Carcinoma arising from anal fistula
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with abdominal distension and pain of 10 days’ duration. No vomiting or obstipation was seen.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen was performed. Small bowel loops appeared dilated with multiple air-fluid levels (Figures 1, 2a, and 2b). A transition zone was found on the right side of the urinary bladder. A closed-loop obstruction was found with distal ileal loops as content, causing indentation of the right superolateral wall of the urinary bladder (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). A few dilated loops show a small bowel faeces sign (luminal contents resembling colonic faecal matter with gas bubbles). The hernial sac was filled with a minimal amount of fluid. There was no abnormal enhancement of the small bowel loops within the hernial sac. No thickening of the bowel walls was noted. Large bowel loops were collapsed. The cecum appeared normal.

   Pros: Can cause small bowel obstruction; can have hernial sac with bowel loops.
   Cons: Usually located in the groin area (inguinal region), not near the urinary bladder; transition zone not near bladder; no mention of inguinal region involvement; CT shows right side of urinary bladder involvement; less likely.

   Pros: Location matches: right side superolateral bladder wall; transition zone near bladder; closed-loop obstruction due to hernial sac near bladder; minimal fluid in sac; no wall enhancement or thickening suggests no ischemia yet; right bladder wall adjacent to small bowel herniation.

   Pros: Common cause of bowel obstruction.
   Cons: Usually no localized transition zone at bladder side; no mention of adhesions in CT; adhesions typically do not produce a closed-loop obstruction with hernial sac; no hernial sac described.

   Pros: Can cause obstruction and transition zones.
   Cons: No mention of bands or mass lesions causing obstruction; no mention of hernial sac; location and imaging description not typical for bands.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old presented with dysphagia and post-prandial vomiting since one month. She had a history of accidental corrosive ingestion 20 years back, for which she was managed surgically (an elective retrosternal oesophageal bypass surgery using a gastric pull-through procedure was performed). She underwent bougie dilatation of the esophagogastric anastomosis for similar presenting symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Frontal chest radiograph showed a fairly well-defined, round soft tissue opacity in the retrocardiac region with a broad base towards the mediastinum (Figure 1). There were no calcifications or cavitation within the opacity. The lateral border of the descending thoracic aorta was obscured by the lesion. On esophagogram, the neo-oesophagus (pulled up stomach) was seen in the right paramedian retrosternal region (Figures 2a and 2b). There was no opacification of the native oesophagus. Unenhanced chest CT showed passage of oral contrast through the neo-oesophagus into the stomach. There was a well-defined fluid-filled elongated cystic lesion (max. diameter of 6.8 cm) in the posterior mediastinum, posterior to the heart and anterior to the descending thoracic aorta. It was communicating with the narrowed proximal and mid native oesophagus (Figures 3a and 3b).

2) Oesophageal duplication cyst  
3) Post-operative collection  
4) Pancreatic pseudocyst  

The finding of a well-defined fluid-filled lesion communicating with a narrowed, blind-ended native oesophagus in a patient with previous bypass surgery and corrosive injury strongly supports the diagnosis of oesophageal mucocele rather than duplication cyst or postoperative collection (which would be expected earlier and more inflammatory). OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We report a case of a 16-year-old patient living in a rural environment. The symptomatology goes back to 3 months with diffuse abdominal pain, distension, and with vomiting and fever 3 days before his admission to the ER. Clinical examination revealed a slight dullness of the flanks and hypogastrium.
 Imaging Findings: An abdominal ultrasound was performed and revealed multiple hepatic, splenic and peritoneal cystic lesions with large intraperitoneal effusion (Figure 1). Abdominal and pelvic CT scan in axial, sagittal and coronal sections showed multiple cystic lesions adherent to the peritoneal leaflets, falciform ligament, great omentum and mesentery (Figures 2 and 4). The largest cyst is located in the left iliac fossa, measuring 55.5 x 42 mm, containing some calcifications (Figure 3), and with a large intraperitoneal effusion. The CT scan also revealed hepatic and splenic cystic lesions.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old female presented with progressively worsening holocranial headache over 3 years, occasionally accompanied by photophobia and phonophobia, sometimes waking her at night, and refractory to mild analgesics. She denied visual symptoms, sinus surgery, or head trauma. Ear, nose, and throat (ENT) examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed complete opacification of the left sphenoid sinus by a hyperattenuating lesion, with moderate expansion and smooth thinning and remodelling of its osseous walls (Figure 1a). Calcifications were present within the lesion (Figures 1b and 3). A focal dehiscence of the sinus roof in close proximity to the anterior cranial fossa floor and sclerosis of the posterior and lateral walls of the sinus were seen (Figure 1b and 1c).
 
 On MRI, the lesion was iso to mildly hypointense on T1-W (Figure 2a) and markedly hypointense on T2-W (Figure 2b). Restricted diffusion was observed in the right sphenoid sinus on DWI/ADC (Figure 4a). Post-contrast T1-W imaging revealed a smooth peripheral rim of enhancement (Figure 5). Additionally, a thin dural enhancement was noted contiguous to the erosion of the sinus roof. The adjacent brain parenchyma appeared normal, with no signs of oedema or infiltration.

   - Pros: Hyperattenuating lesion with internal calcifications on CT is typical for fungal balls; smooth expansion and remodeling of sinus walls is supportive; restricted diffusion can be seen due to thick fungal debris; peripheral rim enhancement is common; dural enhancement due to inflammation adjacent to bone erosion can occur.    - Cons: Usually confined to the sinus without bone destruction, but remodeling and mild bone thinning can be seen; focal dehiscence of the sinus roof can be seen but invasive features (such as brain infiltration) are absent.  
2) Isolated chronic rhinosinusitis:  
   - Pros: Opacification of sinus possible; bone remodeling can occur over long-standing disease.  
   - Cons: Internal hyperattenuation and calcifications are unusual; restricted diffusion is not typical; peripheral rim enhancement and dural reaction are not classical; bone erosion with dehiscence is uncommon in non-invasive sinusitis.

- Invasive fungal rhinosinusitis would be expected to have more aggressive signs, such as brain infiltration, edema, patient immunosuppressed, or rapid progression, absent here.

- Isolated chronic rhinosinusitis lacks characteristic hyperattenuating lesion and calcifications and bone erosion.

The thin dural enhancement adjacent to bone dehiscence could be reactive inflammation.  
</analysis>

Sphenoid sinus mycetoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 32-year-old, 12-week postpartum female presented with an incidental finding of bilaterally enlarged ovaries on ultrasound. The patient was asymptomatic at the time of presentation, with no associated abdominal pain, fever, or systemic symptoms. There was no significant personal or family history of malignancy or other chronic illnesses.
 Imaging Findings: On the initial ultrasound, both ovaries were massively enlarged (right 444 cm³, left 120 cm³) and displayed a heterogeneous echo texture with hypervascularity. No significant free fluid or adnexal torsion was noted.
 
 On contrast-enhanced whole-body MRI, bilateral ovarian masses exhibited scattered bulky lesions with significant diffusion restriction and contrast enhancement, without signs of necrosis or haemorrhage (Figure 1). Additional bulky lesions were identified in the breasts, mesentery, intestinal serosa, peritoneum, and the wall of the terminal ileum.
 
 PET-CT demonstrated intense fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (SUV max. 14 g/ml) in the same regions identified on MRI, confirming metabolic activity consistent with malignancy (Figure 2).
 
 Breast imaging (ultrasound and mammography) revealed numerous sharply circumscribed, hypervascular, hypoechoic masses in both breasts (Figure 3). The biggest lesion measured 140 cm³. Mammography confirmed these findings, identifying well-defined, radiodense masses without calcifications or architectural distortion (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Taking account of all the imaging features together, these lesions were categorised as BIRADS 5.

A) Key findings:  
- Young, 32-year-old postpartum female, asymptomatic.  
- Bilateral massive ovarian enlargement with heterogeneous texture and hypervascularity.  
- Additional bulky, hypervascular masses in breasts, mesentery, intestinal serosa, peritoneum, and terminal ileum wall.  
- Strong diffusion restriction and contrast enhancement on MRI, no necrosis or hemorrhage.  
- Intense FDG uptake on PET-CT in all these areas, consistent with high metabolic activity and malignancy.  
- Breast lesions sharply circumscribed, hypoechoic, hypervascular, well-defined without calcifications or architectural distortion; BIRADS 5 (highly suspicious for malignancy).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Metastatic disease: Pro - Multiple bulky lesions with FDG avidity suggest metastatic spread; Cons - No primary cancer identified; presence of symmetrical bilateral ovarian masses in a young woman more typical of lymphoma.  
- Primary ovarian malignancy: Pro - Bilateral massive ovarian masses; Cons - Primary ovarian cancers rarely present in such young patients with symmetric bilateral masses and simultaneous widespread extracostal involvement (breasts, intestines, mesentery) at diagnosis.  
- Burkitt lymphoma: Pro - Burkitt lymphoma commonly presents with fast-growing, bulky masses, often involving abdomen, pelvis, and sometimes breasts; typical in young patients; hypervascular, FDG avid masses with diffusion restriction; multisite involvement matches the pattern; breasts are an unusual but possible site; aggressive and symmetrical bilaterally. Cons - Burkitt lymphoma usually more typical in males and young children but can occur in young adults, especially postpartum due to hormonal changes.  
- Other aggressive lymphoma: Pro - Aggressive lymphoma could explain multisite bulky lesions; Cons - No specific subtype identified; breast involvement is rare but not exclusive.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Burkitt lymphoma - Most fits the pattern of a fast-growing, hypervascular, FDG avid, multinodular disease involving abdomen/pelvis and breasts in a young adult; bilateral symmetric ovarian involvement is characteristic.  
2) Other aggressive lymphoma - Similar features but less specific; no direct features distinguishing from Burkitt.  
3) Primary ovarian malignancy - Unlikely given age, bilateral symmetry, and extensive multisite involvement without primary solid organ carcinoma.  
4) Metastatic disease - No primary identified; breast masses and intestines involved suggest lymphoid rather than epithelial origin.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 9-year-old child presented with a complaint of intermittent lower abdominal pain and nocturnal enuresis for two months. There were no gastrointestinal issues, weight loss, asthenia, or lower urinary tract symptoms. The patient had no significant past medical history. During the physical examination, a palpable mass with a hard consistency and non-mobile nature was detected on the left flank. Laboratory studies were normal, including urine catecholamine levels. Tumour markers, including hCG, AFP, and CEA, were also assessed and found to be within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound revealed a solid mass inferior to the left kidney and anterior to the psoas muscle (Figure 1a). The mass was vascularised (Figure 1b), had smooth edges, was slightly heterogeneous, predominantly isoechogenic, with some echogenic foci inside, and measured 5 x 5 x 11 cm in the axial, anteroposterior, and longitudinal axes (Figure 1c).
 
 Further MRI studies (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a retroperitoneal mass inferior to the left kidney and between the left psoas and quadratus lumborum muscles. While there was no evidence of invasion into adjacent structures, it did result in anterior shifting of the psoas muscle. The lesion exhibited an intermediate and heterogeneous signal on T2-weighted images and a low signal on T1-weighted images (Figure 3), with no restriction on the diffusion study (Figures 4a and 4b). The dynamic study (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d) presented heterogeneous and progressive uptake by the contrast product, being more evident in the later phase.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male, with no prior relevant medical history, presents with atraumatic intermittent sharp pain in the lower segment of the right anterior thoracic wall. The symptoms began two months earlier when the patient started exercising (CrossFit), mostly triggered by training (especially abdominal crunches) whilst being asymptomatic in normal daily activities.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging of the costochondral region of the patient showed a normal arrangement of ribs and rib cartilage while the patient was in a supine position (Figure 1). No abnormalities in bone or cartilage contours, nor any soft tissue lesions were identified.
 
 Dynamic ultrasound of the symptomatic area, performed while the patient replicated the movement that elicits pain (abdominal crunches), revealed hypermobility of the 9th rib cartilage, which moved upwards and became pinned beneath the 8th rib cartilage (Figures 2 and 3). This triggered the pain, with a popping sensation associated. When the patient returned to the supine position, the 9th rib returned to its normal position (Figure 4).

 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) revealed multiple areas of nodular thickening in the root of the mesentery and throughout the peritoneum (Figures 1 and 2). There are also signs of invasion of the abdominal wall. Small volume of ascites was identified in the parietocolic gutters and in the rectouterine pouch (Figures 3, 4, and 5).

1. Abdominopelvic actinomycosis - Most consistent with IUD-associated nodular peritoneal and abdominal wall involvement with systemic infection signs.
2. Peritoneal tuberculosis - Could explain inflammatory findings but less compatible with IUD and abdominal wall invasion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old female presents to the emergency department with disorientation. No neurological focality is observed. The patient has a history of migraine and blurred vision in the past year. No other relevant medical background.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows loss of grey-white matter differentiation in the left parietal lobe and hypointensity in the white matter (Figures 1a and 1b), which indicates a stroke lesion in the left parietal lobe. In CT with contrast (Figures 2a and 2b), stenosis of the right middle cerebral artery (arrow), a filiform course of the left middle cerebral artery and extensive collateral circulation in the region are seen (arrowheads).
 
 Angiogram of the left cerebral circulation (Figures 3a and 3b) confirms the presence of significant vascular collateralisation around the circle of Willis (arrows), giving the classic “puff of smoke” appearance. An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side (Figures 3c and 3d) shows similar findings.
 
 FLAIR-weighted (Figure 4a) and T1 post-contrast (Figure 4b) images show cortical hyperintensity and cortical enhancement, respectively, in the left parietal region (arrows).
PostDescription:

Angiogram of the left cerebral circulation confirms the presence of significant vascular collateralisation around the circle of Willis (arrows), giving the classic “puff of smoke” appearance. An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side shows similar findings. FLAIR-weighted and T1 post-contrast images show cortical hyperintensity and cortical enhancement, respectively, in the left parietal region (arrows).
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Central nervous system vasculitides, Systemic lupus erythematosus, Moyamoya disease, Neurofibromatosis type I, Cranial radiotherapy, Atherosclerosis
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 25-year-old female, acute neurologic symptoms (disorientation), history of migraine and blurred vision.  
- Imaging:  
  - CT shows loss of grey-white matter differentiation and hypointensity in left parietal lobe indicating acute stroke.  
  - CT angiography shows stenosis of the right middle cerebral artery, a filiform (thin) course of the left middle cerebral artery, and extensive collateral circulation bilaterally around the circle of Willis.  
  - "Puff of smoke" collateral vessels are noted on cerebral angiogram.  
  - Irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses on both sides.  
  - FLAIR and T1 post-contrast images reveal cortical hyperintensity and enhancement in the left parietal region.  

These findings are classic for Moyamoya disease, which is characterized by progressive stenosis or occlusion of the bilateral internal carotid arteries or proximal portions of the anterior or middle cerebral arteries, leading to the formation of a network of small collateral vessels that appear as a "puff of smoke" on angiography. The vascular irregularities and bilateral nature support this diagnosis. The age and female sex also fit the typical epidemiology. Although CNS vasculitides and atherosclerosis can cause vascular stenoses and strokes, they do not produce the characteristic "puff of smoke" collateral network. Systemic lupus erythematosus can cause vasculitis but again lacks this angiographic appearance. Neurofibromatosis type I and cranial radiotherapy can cause vascular abnormalities but are less likely given clinical history and specific imaging findings.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Central nervous system vasculitides  
   - Pros: Can present with stroke and vascular stenosis.  
   - Cons: Does not typically produce "puff of smoke" collaterals; usually focal and asymmetric involvement.  
2) Systemic lupus erythematosus  
   - Pros: Could lead to vasculitis/stroke.  
   - Cons: "Puff of smoke" collaterals are not characteristic; no systemic lupus signs reported.  
3) Moyamoya disease  
   - Pros: Classic "puff of smoke" appearance on angiogram, bilateral vascular stenoses, young female patient, stroke symptoms.  
   - Cons: None notable; findings perfectly match.  
4) Neurofibromatosis type I  
   - Pros: Could cause vascular lesions.  
   - Cons: No cutaneous stigmata or other clinical findings supporting; imaging does not fit classic vasculature changes of NF1.  
5) Cranial radiotherapy  
   - Pros: Can cause radiation-induced vasculopathies.  
   - Cons: No history of radiotherapy; imaging findings are classic for Moyamoya.  
6) Atherosclerosis  
   - Pros: Can cause stenosis and stroke.  
   - Cons: Rare at this young age; no "puff of smoke" collaterals; atherosclerosis is usually focal and not symmetric.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Moyamoya disease (most consistent with classic "puff of smoke" collaterals, bilateral stenoses, young female patient)  
2) Central nervous system vasculitides  
3) Atherosclerosis  
4) Systemic lupus erythematosus  
5) Neurofibromatosis type I  
6) Cranial radiotherapy  

<final>Moyamoya disease</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old man presented with a history of weakness in the right arm since a few weeks. There was a history of a road traffic accident two months back.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus showed a pre-fixed plexus with supply from the C4 nerve root. There was diffuse oedema in the C4–C6 nerve roots with thickening of the C5 and C6 nerve roots and the superior trunk (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2c). The nerve roots were hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky as compared to the contralateral side (Figures 2a and 2b). There was oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation oedema in the deltoid and the teres minor muscles (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration is also noted in the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.

3. Anatomical variations: pre-fixed plexus with supply from the C4 nerve root.
4. Diffuse oedema and thickening of C4âC6 nerve roots, particularly C5 and C6 and the superior trunk.
5. T2 hyperintensity and bulkiness of the affected nerve roots compared to the opposite side.
6. Oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course.
7. Subacute denervation oedema in deltoid and teres minor muscles (muscles innervated by the axillary nerve and superior trunk).
8. Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration in supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles (innervated by suprascapular nerve, branch of the superior trunk).
9. Clinical correlation with weakness in the right arm consistent with superior trunk involvement.

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1â2 pros/cons:
1. Traumatic brachial plexopathy:
   - Pros: History of road traffic accident; nerve thickening and oedema consistent with trauma; subacute denervation changes consistent with injury.
   - Cons: None significant; findings match trauma sequelae well.
2. Brachial plexitis:
   - Pros: Nerve thickening and T2 hyperintensity could be seen with inflammation.
   - Cons: Traumatic history is more consistent; brachial plexitis often has more uniform inflammation without trauma history; no mention of preceding infection or idiopathic onset.

   - Pros: Can involve upper brachial plexus.
   - Cons: Usually chronic; may show nerve compression signs but not diffuse oedema and thickening; no typical trauma correlation; not acute or subacute; no mention of compression point or vascular involvement.

2. Brachial plexitis - less likely due to trauma history and discrete nerve root/plexus involvement rather than uniform inflammation.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented with a gradually enlarging swelling over the anterior aspect of the right ankle, first noticed two years earlier. He reported no pain, trauma, functional limitation, or gait disturbance. On examination, a ~5 cm, well-circumscribed, mobile, and non-tender soft tissue mass was observed, not adherent to deep planes and without skin changes or signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound demonstrated a well-circumscribed soft tissue mass measuring 33 × 28 × 14 mm (L × AP × T) within the subcutaneous tissue, completely enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon. The lesion exhibited a heterogeneous echotexture, predominantly iso-echoic relative to the surrounding fat (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 Subsequent MRI revealed a fusiform, encapsulated mass located within the subcutaneous plane, anterior to the aponeurosis, and circumferentially enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon (Figure 3). The lesion appeared hyperintense on T1-weighted sequences with multiple internal hypointense septations (Figure 3), showed high-signal intensity on T2-weighted gradient-echo sequences (Figure 4), and demonstrated marked hypointensity on fat-suppressed proton density-weighted images (Figures 5 and 6). Across all sequences, the signal characteristics closely resembled those of subcutaneous adipose tissue, consistent with a lipomatous composition.

No evidence of blooming on FFE images or restriction on DWI and ADC maps. Abnormal T1WI hyperintensity noted within the globus pallidus (Figure 4).
 
 USG of the abdomen revealed altered echotexture of the liver with hepatosplenomegaly, consistent with chronic liver parenchymal disease /(Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 88-year-old patient presented with an incidental lung mass. A computed tomography-guided biopsy and a bronchoscopy were performed, but the results were inconclusive. Given the size and characteristics of the lesion, radiotherapy was considered. The patient was asymptomatic and exhibited a normal clinical examination. The third biopsy yielded diagnostic results.
 Imaging Findings: Conventional chest X-ray shows an enlarged right pulmonary hilum and magnification of the cardiac silhouette due to the superimposed right lower lobe mass (silhouette sign) (Figure 1).
 
 Computed tomography (CT) with a soft tissue window shows a right lower lobe mass with an approximate diameter of 38 x 36 x 66 mm, with ill-defined borders and calcifications within (Figure 2). The pulmonary window shows atelectasis adjacent to the mass, as well as bronchial contact without complete collapse (Figure 4). PET/CT (positron emission tomography) shows FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) fixation in the lesion, suggesting malignancy (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior. Surgeons requested a contrast-enhanced CT scan to clarify the diagnosis.
 Imaging Findings: The contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed dilated small bowel loops with an apparent calibre change in the lower quadrant of the abdomen (Figure 1). As no masses or bowel thickening were identified, the diagnosis was consistent with an incomplete small bowel obstruction, likely due to abdominal adhesions—a diagnosis of exclusion. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior.
 
 Additionally, the gastric wall showed thickened gastric folds (Figure 2). Subsequent gastroscopy confirmed the thickened folds, and biopsies were obtained (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Histopathological examination revealed atrophic gastritis and tumoural cells positive for GATA 3, mammaglobin, and oestrogen receptors.
 
 Both conventional (Figures 4a and 4b) and contrast-enhanced mammography (Figures 5a and 5b) demonstrated bilateral diffuse masses with non-mass enhancement. Palpable areas were biopsied in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast—4 o’clock—(Figure 6a) and the junction of the outer quadrants of the left breast—9 o’clock—(Figure 6b). No axillary lymphadenopathy was detected (Figures 6c and 6d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old female came with complaints of a slowly growing suprapubic mass since a few months and lower abdominal discomfort. She was further advised to undergo ultrasound imaging and subsequently CT abdomen for renal anomaly, and MRI pelvis for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: MRI pelvis reveals a large tubular T1 hyperintense structure in the pelvis on the right side, consisting of haemorrhagic contents in an enlarged hourglass-shaped right uterine horn and cervix. The left uterine horn appears unremarkable. CT abdomen (Figures 1 and 2) reveals an empty right renal fossa with a cross-fused kidney seen on the left. No obvious free fluid or adnexal lesion is seen.
PostDescription:

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient age: 13-year-old female.  
- Clinical presentation: slowly growing suprapubic mass and lower abdominal discomfort.  
- Imaging: MRI pelvis shows a large tubular T1 hyperintense structure on the right side consisting of hemorrhagic contents in an enlarged hourglass-shaped right uterine horn and cervix. The left uterine horn is unremarkable.  
- CT abdomen shows empty right renal fossa and a cross-fused kidney on the left (renal agenesis on right side with crossed fused renal anomaly).  
- No free fluid or adnexal mass.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Müllerian duct anomaly:  
   - Pro: The presence of abnormal uterine morphology (hourglass-shaped right uterine horn) suggests Müllerian duct anomaly.  
   - Con: Müllerian duct anomalies encompass a broad range of structural abnormalities; this alone does not specify the subtype, and the hemorrhagic contents and obstructed horn are not specifically described.  

2) OHVIRA / Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome:  
   - Pro: Classic presentation includes uterus didelphys (double uterus or duplication of uterus), obstructed hemivagina on one side containing bloody content (hematohemivagina), and ipsilateral renal agenesis. Our case matches well: right side has an enlarged, obstructed (hemorrhagic) uterine horn and cervix (hematohemivagina), and empty right renal fossa (renal agenesis). The hourglass shape suggests uterine didelphys (duplicated uterus) with obstruction.  
   - Con: None significant; findings are classic for OHVIRA.  

3) Endometrioma:  
   - Pro: T1 hyperintense hemorrhagic contents could be endometrioma.  
   - Con: Endometriomas are ovarian cystic masses, usually unilateral, and here the lesion is tubular, in the uterine horn and cervix, not the ovary. Also, uterus horn involvement is atypical. No adnexal mass seen.  

4) Ovarian cystic mass:  
   - Pro: Cystic mass can present, but imaging shows no adnexal lesion.  
   - Con: No free fluid or adnexal lesion seen; lesion is tubular and uterine in location, not ovarian.  

C) Ranking:  
1) OHVIRA / Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome - pathognomonic for uterus didelphys, obstructed hemivagina with hemorrhagic contents, and ipsilateral renal agenesis. This completely fits the imaging and clinical scenario.  
2) Müllerian duct anomaly - umbrella term, true, but less specific.  
3) Endometrioma - less likely, location inconsistent.  
4) Ovarian cystic mass - inconsistent with imaging findings.  

Thus, the most accurate diagnosis is OHVIRA / Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome.
</analysis>  
<final>  
OHVIRA / Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old male with one week of sudden-onset fever, abdominal pain, cervicalgia, myalgia, palpitations and shortness of breath. Recent history of travel to Africa for 1 month, and medications for malaria prophylaxis and pre-exposure prophylaxis for HIV. On examination, he had a fever of 38,2°C, tachycardia and right lower quadrant abdominal tenderness. Initial workup was non-contributory, with normal CBC, BMP, chest x-ray, and abdomen ultrasound. With wide differentials, including endemic, infectious and neoplastic conditions, CT of the head, neck and chest was performed and was unrevealing. Serology and lumbar puncture were also performed and were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed, and revealed a 3.7 x 3.1 x 5.1 cm, avidly enhancing paraduodenal mass with central calcification (Figures 1a and 1b), presumed to be an enlarged lymph node. Adjacent organs, including the bowel, were unremarkable. There was no lymphadenopathy elsewhere. An endoscopic ultrasound was done (Figures 2a and 2b) to obtain an aspiration biopsy that revealed B-cell follicles with no evidence of malignancy. After discussion, the patient opted for watchful waiting. A follow-up CT done after 4 months demonstrated no significant change in size or appearance of the mass. This was subsequently excised, and histopathology revealed the final diagnosis.

- No significant change in size over 4 months.  
- Definitive histopathology after excision gave final diagnosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Sarcoma:  
- Pros: Can present as a solitary, enhancing mass; can be vascular.  
- Cons: Usually solid mass without central calcification typical for lymphoma or Castleman; rare in paraduodenal region; B-cell follicles on biopsy argue against sarcoma; no change over 4 months less likely aggressive sarcoma.  

2) Lymphoma:  
- Pros: Enlarged lymph node with enhancement; B-cell follicles could fit; common in abdomen.  
- Cons: Usually multiple nodes or more widespread disease; no distant lymphadenopathy; biopsy negative for malignancy; stability over 4 months less typical for lymphoma; lack of systemic symptoms beyond febrile episode and no malignancy on biopsy argue against.  

3) Unicentric Castleman disease:  
- Pros: Typically presents as a solitary, avidly enhancing lymph node mass; paraduodenal location possible; central calcification sometimes seen; B-cell follicles on biopsy consistent; stable size over months; surgical excision is curative.  
- Cons: Rare but characteristic presentation fits well (solitary, enhancing, B-cell follicles).  

4) Gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST):  
- Pros: Hypervascular mass; can occur near duodenum.  
- Cons: Usually arises from bowel wall rather than lymph node region; biopsy would show spindle or epithelioid cells, not B-cell follicles; central calcification unusual; no change over months less likely for GIST.  

5) Neuroendocrine tumour:  
- Pros: Hypervascular mass; can be in small bowel/mesenteric region.  
- Cons: Usually multiple small liver metastases or thickened mesentery, not isolated lymph node; biopsy would show neuroendocrine cells; central calcification rare; B-cell follicles not consistent.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Unicentric Castleman disease (best fit: solitary enhancing lymph node with B-cell follicles, stable over months, no malignancy).  
2) Lymphoma (similar but biopsy negative for malignancy and no other nodes).  
3) Sarcoma (less likely due to site and biopsy).  
4) Neuroendocrine tumour (unlikely due to biopsy and typical features).  
5) Gastrointestinal stromal tumour (least likely due to biopsy and location).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old woman had unintended weight loss and anaemia for several months. There was no history of bowel or urinary changes, and an endoscopy was negative. A CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed to evaluate for malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in a portal venous phase (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). An ovoid, heterogeneous soft tissue mass with smooth, well-defined margins was present in the left upper mesorectal space, mildly displacing the rectum to the right. It measured 61 x 58 x 45 mm and did not contain cystic spaces or macroscopic fat. The mass did not herniate through the sciatic notch and did not appear along the expected path of a nerve or vasculature. Upon administration of IV contrast, the mass was hypervascular on the portal venous phase. There was free fluid in the pelvis, representing simple ascites, without evidence of complexity or active bleeding. No MRI was performed since the patient was lost to follow-up after her initial CT scan and biopsy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 17-year history of diabetes mellitus presented to our institution with a 3-month history of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms, including urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, and gross haematuria. Notably absent was acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. After hospitalisation, it was found that there were pneumaturia and faeces-like changes in the urine (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal ultrasonography showed a gas-like strong echo and irregular hyperechoic stone-like mass with posterior shadowing in the bladder (Figure 2a). To exclude gynaecological diseases, a transvaginal ultrasound was performed. The right lateral wall of the bladder and the adjacent bowel were connected through a fistula. The fistula tract was about 3.8 cm long and 0.58 cm wide, characterised as a central hyperechoic and peripheral hypoechoic tract on sonogram (Figure 2b). These ultrasound findings supported the diagnosis of vesico-enteric fistula, but the specific part of the intestine could not be clarified.
 
 A computed tomography (CT) scan showed a small air-fluid level at the top of the bladder (Figure 3a). A tubular tract could be seen protruding from the right lateral wall of the bladder up to the neighbouring thickened bowel and this is filled with contrast-opacified urine (Figure 3b). A diagnosis of fistulous communication between the bladder and bowel was suggested following the findings on the excretory-phase CT scan. CT coronal scan showed faecal stones in the bladder (Figure 3c).
 
 Cystoscopy showed an oedematous right lateral wall of the bladder associated with inflammatory changes. It also revealed a fistulous tract opening with a diameter of 1 cm, with pus moss and yellow floc attached to the bladder mucosal surface. The yellow floc is the stone-like mass seen on the ultrasound. The cystoscope could be inserted via a sinus tract opening in the right lateral bladder wall, where the distal tip exhibited bifurcation. Despite attempts, the endoscope could only advance 2 cm beyond the entry point, with further progression prevented by this structural anomaly.

   - Pros: Post-ablation fistulae or obstetric trauma can cause bladder-vagina fistulas.  
   - Cons: No mention of vaginal bleeding or vaginal fluid; transvaginal ultrasound was normal; the fistula is to the bowel, not the vagina; no gynecological findings to support this.

C) Ranking:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

   - Pros: Age, smoker, restrictive pattern, subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing with apicobasal gradient, sharp interface, typical imaging pattern, no ground glass or inflammation favor IPF.  
   - Cons: No explicit mention of usual interstitial pneumonia distribution besides apicobasal gradient and subpleural predominance, but findings are classic for IPF.

   - Cons: NSIP usually has basally prevalent ground-glass opacities and less honeycombing. Here, honeycombing is exuberant and extensive, no ground glass noted. The sharp transition and extensive honeycombing argue against NSIP.  

4) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP):  
   - Pros: Rarely seen but can cause interstitial disease.  
   - Cons: Typically in younger patients, associated with autoimmune or immunodeficiency. Imaging shows ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodules, cysts; none present here. Honeycombing and sharp apicobasal transition uncommon.  

C) Ranking:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old woman with a longstanding history of rheumatoid factor-positive rheumatoid arthritis (RA) was being treated with subcutaneous methotrexate 10 mg. She went to the emergency department due to sudden dyspnoea and left chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray showed a moderate left pneumothorax and a cavitated lesion in the subpleural region of the left upper lobe (LUL) (Figure 1a), which was present on a prior chest X-ray (Figure 1b). After a chest tube was placed (Figure 1c), the pneumothorax did not fully resolve, and symptoms persisted, prompting a CT scan.
 
 CT reported a left pneumothorax. The tube was correctly positioned. A cavitated nodule located in the LUL with communication to the pleural cavity was shown in axial (Figure 2a) and coronal planes (Figure 2b). Other broad-based nodules in the pleural surface were identified (Figure 2c). The rest of the parenchyma presented a slight thickening of the interlobular septa with a diffuse bilateral distribution, probably related to RA-associated interstitial lung disease.
 
 A subsequent revaluation, after reviewing the patient’s radiological history, concluded that the image of a cavitated nodule (Figures 2a and 2b) corresponded to the lesion visible in the previous X-ray (Figure 1b), and proposed the differential diagnosis between cavitated lung neoplasia, rheumatoid nodule and other cavitary lesions such as tuberculosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old woman presented to the gastroenterologist with postprandial fullness and nausea persisting for one year, along with a 4 kg weight loss. She had no abdominal pain and reported normal faeces. Blood tests were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), a suspicion of a thickened stomach wall was raised.
 
 CT (Figures 2a and 2b) confirmed diffuse wall thickening and hyperaemic inner lining of the stomach with a small lumen. Lymphadenopathies were identified in the gastrohepatic and gastrocolic ligaments. No peritoneal metastases were observed on CT, but the lack of significant intra-abdominal fat limited definitive exclusion.
 
 During gastroscopy Figure 3), insufflation of the stomach was difficult, but no focal tumoral process was noted.
 
 Initial MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) showed diffusion restriction of the stomach lining. Additionally, multiple subtle peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions were identified, which were confirmed via diagnostic laparoscopy. Follow-up MRI (Figures 5a and 5b) showed disease progression with increased wall thickening of the stomach in the antrum, pylorus, and duodenal bulb, as well as the development of bilateral ovarian lesions.

FinalDiagnosis: Linitis plastica in diffuse stomach carcinoma

A) Key findings:
- 40-year-old woman with postprandial fullness, nausea, and weight loss (4 kg) over one year.
- Blood tests normal.
- Ultrasound: thickened stomach wall.
- CT: diffuse stomach wall thickening, hyperaemic inner lining, small lumen; lymphadenopathies in gastrohepatic and gastrocolic ligaments; no definitive peritoneal metastases seen (limited by lack of intra-abdominal fat).
- Gastroscopy: difficult insufflation of the stomach, no focal tumoral process.
- Initial MRI: diffusion restriction of the stomach lining.
- Additional findings: multiple subtle peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions confirmed on laparoscopy.
- Follow-up MRI: disease progression with increased wall thickening in antrum, pylorus, and duodenal bulb; bilateral ovarian lesions developed.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Metastases
   - Pros: Presence of peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions could represent metastases; bilateral ovarian lesions could be Krukenberg tumors (metastatic from stomach carcinoma).
   - Cons: No primary mass lesion seen on gastroscopy; no obvious primary tumor; initial CT and gastroscopy did not reveal a focal lesion. 2) Lymphoma
   - Pros: Diffuse stomach wall thickening with hyperaemic inner lining; lymphadenopathies present; lymphoma can cause diffuse infiltration.
   - Cons: Gastric lymphoma usually presents with a more nodular mass or ulcerated lesion; lymphoma usually does not cause peritoneal carcinomatosis or ovarian lesions. Also, lymphoma generally does not cause peritoneal serosal lesions or Krukenberg-like bilateral ovarian lesions.

3) Inflammation
   - Pros: Wall thickening and hyperaemia can be due to inflammation.
   - Cons: Diffuse wall thickening and diffusion restriction are more suggestive of a neoplastic or infiltrative process rather than simple inflammation. Inflammation generally does not produce peritoneal metastases or bilateral ovarian lesions.

4) Ménétrier disease
   - Pros: Diffuse stomach wall thickening with mucosal hyperaemia and small lumen could fit Ménétrier disease. Hypersecretory state and hypoalbuminemia are typical, but no hypoalbuminemia or protein loss reported.
   - Cons: Ménétrier disease is rare, usually presents with protein loss and edema. Also, Ménétrier disease does not typically cause lymphadenopathy, peritoneal lesions, or bilateral ovarian involvement. Diffusion restriction is not classic for Ménétrier disease.

   - Pros: Diffuse wall thickening, hyperaemic inner lining, small stomach lumen, and stiff stomach wall (felt during gastroscopy as difficulty insufflating) are classic for linitis plastica.    - Cons: No focal tumoral lesion seen endoscopically, but linitis plastica often shows a rigid, leather bottle stomach with little or no endoscopic visible lesion. This matches.

3. Lymphoma (possible but less common for peritoneal lesions and ovarian metastases)
4. Ménétrier disease (unlikely given metastases and lymphadenopathies)
5. Inflammation (least likely)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male patient presented to the emergency room with visual disturbances and right-hand weakness. He had a medical history of alcoholism, tobacco use, hyperlipidaemia, and traumatic splenectomy, and was hospitalised due to an ischemic stroke. He underwent routine diagnostics, where, from laboratory tests, only hyperlipidaemia was clinically significant.
 Imaging Findings: Routine abdominal ultrasound revealed an asymptomatic, well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechogenic mass measuring 46 x 36 mm in the 8th liver segment (Figure 1).
 
 On abdominal multidetector computerised tomography (MDCT), the mass was ill-defined and isodense to the surrounding liver parenchyma on unenhanced scans (Figure 2a). Following intravenous contrast, the lesion demonstrated increased attenuation (Figure 2b), whereas, in the latter phases, it was isodense with the surrounding liver parenchyma (Figure 2c). Other findings included two splenunculi, one near the lesser curvature of the stomach (Figure 2d) and one in the left subphrenic region (Figure 2e).
 
 Contrast abdominal MR scans confirmed a well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense mass measuring 43 x 30 mm (Figure 3a). The mass showed poor uniform post-contrast enhancement in the arterial phase (Figure 3b), following washout in later phases (Figure 3c). The mass showed significant restricted diffusion on DWI (Figure 3d) and low ADC values (Figure 3e).

Following intravenous contrast, the lesion demonstrated increased attenuation, whereas, in the latter phases, it was isodense with the surrounding liver parenchyma. Other findings included two splenunculi, one near the lesser curvature of the stomach and one in the left subphrenic region. Contrast abdominal MR scans confirmed a well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense mass measuring 43 x 30 mm. The mass showed poor uniform post-contrast enhancement in the arterial phase, following washout in later phases. The mass showed significant restricted diffusion on DWI and low ADC values.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 62-year-old male with history of stroke and risk factors (alcoholism, smoking, hyperlipidaemia).  
- Imaging incidentally discovered a 46 x 36 mm homogeneously hypoechoic lesion in segment 8 of the liver on ultrasound.  
- MDCT: mass is ill-defined, isodense to liver parenchyma on unenhanced imaging, shows increased attenuation on arterial phase, washout in later phases.  
- MRI: well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense lesion, poor uniform arterial enhancement with washout, significant restricted diffusion and low ADC values.  
- Two splenunculi present: one near lesser curvature of the stomach, one in left subphrenic region, consistent with splenic tissue remnants or heterotopic spleen tissue, usually after splenectomy or traumatic spleen injury.

1. Atypical haemangioma  
   - Pro: Hypoechoic on US; T2 hyperintensity; arterial phase enhancement possible. Typically, haemangiomas show peripheral nodular enhancement and centripetal fill-in. "Atypical" can include early central enhancement but washout is uncommon.  
   - Con: Washout pattern on delayed phases is unusual for haemangiomas; haemangiomas normally do not show washout.  
2. Liver adenoma  
   - Pro: Hypervascular lesion could be adenoma, particularly in patients with risk factors.  
   - Con: Typically, liver adenomas occur in younger females on oral contraceptives; they usually show no washout pattern; restricted diffusion can be variable; no history of estrogen exposure.  
3. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)  
   - Pro: Patient with risk factors (alcohol, age, hyperlipidaemia), lesion shows arterial phase enhancement and washout, classic for HCC. Restricted diffusion common in HCC.  
   - Con: No history of chronic hepatitis or cirrhosis mentioned; but risk factors exist; lesion is well-defined and in segment 8, typical location.  
4. Intrahepatic splenosis  
   - Pro: Known post-splenectomy findings; presence of splenunculi elsewhere suggests ectopic splenic tissue. Intrahepatic splenosis often mimics hypervascular lesions; can show arterial enhancement and washout similar to liver lesions; is isodense on CT and shows restricted diffusion similar to spleen tissue.  
   - Con: Hypoechoic on US could fit; ill-defined on CT sometimes, but well-defined on MRI; presence of splenunculi elsewhere supports theory.

   - Pro: Presence of splenunculi consistent with accessory spleen.  
   - Con: Usually located in typical accessory spleen sites (splenophrenic or gastro-splenic ligaments), not intrahepatically in segment 8. Accessory spleen is usually extrahapatic.  
6. Metastasis  
   - Pro: Metastases can show washout and restricted diffusion.  
   - Con: No known primary cancer; lesion is well-defined single mass; no other sites of disease mentioned.

2. Intrahepatic splenosis: presence of multiple splenunculi and typical imaging features of splenic tissue (arterial enhancement, washout, low ADC). The location is within liver, segment 8, which is a possible site for intraperitoneal splenosis after trauma/splenectomy.  
3. Atypical haemangioma: somewhat less likely due to washout and irregular enhancement.

6. Accessory spleen: less likely due to intrahepatic location and typical location elsewhere.

OriginalDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed:

 Diffusion restriction on DWI with low ADC values (Figures 1d, 1e, 2d, 2e, 3d, 3e, 4d, and 4e).
 
 Additional involvement of the bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex and corticospinal tracts involving the posterior limb of the internal capsule and midbrain.
 
 No post-contrast enhancement (Figures 6a and 6b).

A) Key findings:
- 36-year-old male with chronic alcohol use and acute loss of consciousness.
- MRI findings include symmetrical T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in the corpus callosum.
- The lesions in the corpus callosum are T1 hypointense.
- Diffusion restriction on DWI with corresponding low ADC values suggests acute cytotoxic edema.
- Additional involvement of bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex, corticospinal tracts involving posterior limb of internal capsule, and midbrain.
- No post-contrast enhancement.
- No history of seizures, fever, or vomiting; normal blood tests.
- Vitamin B12 and folate levels not measured.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Wernickeâs encephalopathy
   Pros: Chronic alcohol use predisposes to this; typical regions affected are mammillary bodies, thalamus, periaqueductal gray, hypothalamus.
   Cons: Corpus callosum and fronto-parieto-temporal cortex involvement are atypical; no mention of mammillary body involvement or classic locations; no contrast enhancement; no vomiting or fever.

2) Osmotic demyelination syndrome
   Pros: Involves central pons and sometimes other brain areas; symmetric lesions.
   Cons: Typically involves central pons ("trident sign"), can involve basal ganglia, thalami, cortex but corpus callosum involvement and corticospinal tracts without prominent pontine involvement is atypical; no known rapid osmotic shifts reported here.

3) Toxic-metabolic encephalopathy
   Pros: Acute loss of consciousness, diffuse brain involvement possible; can affect frontal lobes.
   Cons: Corpus callosum involvement with diffusion restriction is not typical; no metabolic derangement specified; vitamin deficiencies not ruled out.

4) Acute MarchiafavaâBignami disease
   Pros: Typical in chronic alcohol users; mainly involves corpus callosum with T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity; diffusion restriction common in acute phase; can extend to adjacent white matter tracts and cortex; involvement of corticospinal tracts and midbrain is reported; symmetrical pattern fits.
   Cons: Acute presentation fits clinical scenario.

5) Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy
   Pros: Can cause cortical and deep gray matter diffusion restriction; common in loss of consciousness.
   Cons: Corpus callosum involvement is unusual; typical patterns involve basal ganglia, thalami, cortex in watershed areas but not specifically corpus callosum symmetrical lesions with diffuse callosal involvement and corticospinal tracts.

1. Acute MarchiafavaâBignami disease (ABMD) - most consistent with chronic alcoholism, symmetric corpus callosum lesions with diffusion restriction, involvement of corticospinal tracts and adjoining cortex fits typical acute ABMD presentation.
2. Wernickeâs encephalopathy - less likely due to atypical locations and no classic findings.
3. Osmotic demyelination syndrome - less consistent due to absence of typical pontine involvement.
4. Toxic-metabolic encephalopathy - nonspecific, less likely given specific callosal involvement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a history of smoking and occasional alcohol use presented with a foreign body sensation and odynophagia. Clinical examination showed a bulge on the right lateral pharyngeal wall with normal mucosa and palatine tonsil. Neurological exam and lab tests, particularly white blood cell count, SR and RCP, were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The patient underwent an MR scan showing a 20 x 35 x 43.5 mm cystic mass in the right retropharyngeal space (RPS) (Figures 1a and 1b), bulging the prevertebral muscles and impinging on the oropharynx, with leftward deviation and reduced airway patency. The mass was spontaneously hyperintense to muscle on T1W images (Figure 2), hyperintense on T2W images, with a thin septation on T2 FS images (Figure 3). There was no restricted diffusion on the ADC map (Figure 4), and no enhancement was noted on subtracted T1W images before and after gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b). There was no oedema in the surrounding tissues, and no abnormalities were seen in the pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, thyroid, or cervical lymph nodes.
 
 A comparison with an MR scan from 20 years ago showed the lesion was already present but smaller (10 x 20 x 35 mm) (Figure 6). At that time, the MRI was performed for unrelated reasons, and the incidental retropharyngeal lesion was interpreted as a presumed vascular malformation. Since the patient was asymptomatic, follow-up observation and monitoring was implemented, but the patient was lost to follow-up after a few years.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 64-year-old male smoker with right lateral pharyngeal wall bulge and foreign body sensation/odynophagia.  
- On MR, a cystic mass in the right retropharyngeal space, 20 x 35 x 43.5 mm, causing bulging of prevertebral muscles and airway narrowing.  
- MRI characteristics: spontaneously hyperintense on T1W (likely due to proteinaceous or mucinous content), hyperintense on T2W with thin septation, no restricted diffusion, no enhancement post-gadolinium, no surrounding edema.  
- Lesion has been present for at least 20 years, slowly increasing in size.  
- No signs of infection or inflammation (normal labs, no tissue edema, no enhancement).  
- No pharyngeal, laryngeal, thyroid abnormalities or abnormal lymph nodes.  
- No history or clinical signs of malignancy or metastatic disease.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Branchial cyst:  
- Pros: cystic lesion in neck region possible.  
- Cons: Typically located in the lateral neck, anterior to the sternocleidomastoid at the level of the hyoid; not classically in the retropharyngeal space. Usually do not show T1 hyperintensity spontaneously unless proteinaceous content, but location is inconsistent.  
2) Retropharyngeal bronchogenic cyst:  
- Pros: Typically seen in the retropharyngeal space, can be congenital cystic lesions with proteinaceous/mucinous contents causing T1 hyperintensity, no enhancement, no diffusion restriction; slow growth over decades. The location and imaging features (T1 hyperintensity, non-enhancing, no edema) fit well.  
- Cons: None significant; presentation and history fit well.  
3) Ectopic parathyroid cyst:  
- Pros: Possible cystic lesion in the neck.  
- Cons: Usually small, rarely located in the retropharyngeal space; typically do not cause large symptomatic masses; no clinical signs of hyperparathyroidism or neck mass; usually not T1 hyperintense.  
4) Cystic degeneration of a neurogenic tumour:  
- Pros: Could cause cystic mass with internal septation.  
- Cons: Usually show some enhancement; neurogenic tumours can be solid and enhance; T1 hyperintensity less common, and the slow growth over 20 years without clinical symptoms is less typical. Also, location is atypical for neurogenic tumours (usually paraspinal).  
5) Metastatic cystic/necrotic retropharyngeal lymphadenopathy:  
- Pros: Could cause cystic-looking nodes.  
- Cons: No known primary malignancy; lack of enhancement and no surrounding edema/inflammation; no nodal abnormalities; very slow growth over 20 years argues against metastasis.  
6) Suppurative lymphadenopathy:  
- Pros: Could produce cystic/necrotic lymph nodes.  
- Cons: No signs of infection (labs normal), no enhancement, no edema; unlikely for a 20-year slow-growing mass.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- 2) Retropharyngeal bronchogenic cyst: best fit for location, appearance, growth pattern, and lack of inflammation or malignancy.  
- 1) Branchial cyst: less likely due to location.  
- 3) Ectopic parathyroid cyst: less likely due to lack of typical features and location.  
- 4) Cystic degeneration of neurogenic tumour: possible but less likely due to lack of enhancement and atypical site.  
- 5) Metastatic cystic lymphadenopathy and 6) Suppurative lymphadenopathy: unlikely due to clinical course and imaging findings.  

Final choice: Retropharyngeal bronchogenic cyst
</analysis>  
<final>Retropharyngeal bronchogenic cyst</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old male patient with a history of non-metastatic urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer, both successfully treated surgically two years ago. During follow-up, a palpable, hard, and fixed cervical mass was detected. No history of neck trauma or radiation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a 6 cm solid and heterogeneous left mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularisation, not arising from the thyroid gland (Figures 1a and 1b). This mass was displacing and compressing the left internal jugular vein, which was still permeable (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e). Findings were consistent with a calcified adenopathy.
 
 A subsequent non-contrast CT due to renal impairment revealed a solid left supraclavicular mass, with a heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, and thick calcifications. Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed (Figures 2a and 2b). There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level (Figure 2c).
 
 A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue (Figures 3a and 3b).
PostDescription:

This mass was displacing and compressing the left internal jugular vein, which was still permeable. Findings were consistent with a calcified adenopathy. A subsequent non-contrast CT due to renal impairment revealed a solid left supraclavicular mass, with a heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, and thick calcifications. Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed. There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level. A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue.

A) Key findings:
- Patient: 75-year-old male with prior history of urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer, both surgically treated.
- Clinical presentation: palpable, hard, fixed cervical mass.
- Ultrasound: 6 cm solid, heterogeneous left neck mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularisation, not arising from thyroid; compressing but not engulfing internal jugular vein.
- Non-contrast CT: Large left supraclavicular mass, heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, thick calcifications, thickening of lateral cervical musculature ipsilaterally; pathological calcified lymphadenopathy at cervical level Va.
- Histology: Spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, atypical cells with osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue formation.
- No history of radiation or neck trauma.
- Renal impairment precluding contrast CT.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Extraskeletal osteosarcoma
- Pros: Histology shows spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, atypical cells producing osteoid matrix (diagnostic hallmark); internal thick calcifications are consistent; mass is solid and large, consistent with aggressive lesion; no radiation or trauma history needed in extraskeletal form.
- Cons: Rare in the head and neck region but possible; the clinical history of prior malignancies raises concern but does not exclude this.

2) Myositis ossificans
- Pros: Thickening of musculature may imitate lesion.
- Cons: Age, location (deep soft tissue mass, large and heterogeneous), histology is inconsistent since myositis ossificans shows zonal pattern with mature bone peripherally; here, there is spindle cell malignancy with atypia; also, mass compresses but does not arise from muscle.

3) Calcified metastatic lymphadenopathy
- Pros: History of urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer; cervical adenopathy is common site for metastases; calcifications can be present.
- Cons: Histology shows spindle, pleomorphic cells with osteoid and chondroid matrix, which is more typical of sarcoma than carcinoma metastasis; also, metastatic lymph nodes rarely show true osteoid/chondroid matrix formation; no carcinoma cells seen.

4) Benign conditions with calcified lymph nodes (e.g., tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, amyloidosis)
- Pros: Calcified lymph nodes occur.
- Cons: Histology with osteoid/chondroid matrix and spindle cell malignancy rules out benign entities; patient has no systemic symptoms or history supporting these conditions; size and malignant features inconsistent with benign disease.

5) Another type of high-grade sarcoma
- Pros: Histology consistent with sarcoma; spindle cells with osteoid suggest high-grade sarcoma; but if not osteosarcoma, could be undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma with osteoid/chondroid?
- Cons: The presence of osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue is characteristic of osteosarcoma; other sarcomas rarely produce osteoid and chondroid elements; the histology is quite specific.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
1) Extraskeletal osteosarcoma - best match due to histologic hallmark of malignant spindle cells producing osteoid and chondroid matrix, large heterogeneous mass with thick calcifications, no radiation or trauma, consistent with head and neck presentation.
2) Another type of high-grade sarcoma - possible but less likely because of osteoid/chondroid matrix points specifically to osteosarcoma rather than undifferentiated sarcoma.
3) Calcified metastatic lymphadenopathy - ruled out by histology.
4) Myositis ossificans - ruled out by histology and clinical presentation.
5) Benign conditions - ruled out by histology and clinical presentation.

<final>Extraskeletal osteosarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A sixteen-year-old male patient presented with congenital deafness on the left side and normal hearing on the right side. There was a family history of deafness and hearing loss on the father’s side. He experienced relatively few difficulties in daily life. Audiometry showed near-total deafness on the left side and normal thresholds on the right.
 Imaging Findings: A 3T MR study of the temporal bones was performed. The thin, heavily T2-weighted images revealed a bud-like, cystic cochlea on the left side with absent normal turns, modiolus and interscalar septa (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 6). Additionally, there was no identifiable left cochlear nerve, and apart from the facial nerve, there was only one other nerve, smaller in calibre compared to the facial nerve, in the internal auditory canal, consistent with a hypoplastic vestibulocochlear nerve and cochlear nerve aplasia (Figures 3a, 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f). The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose (Figure 2). The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed (Figures 1a, 1b, 3b, 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, 5e, and 5f).

FinalDiagnosis: Cochlear hypoplasia type I (with aplasia of the cochlear nerve)
